stem: An architect must choose between fine-tuning and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) so an assistant can answer from a large, frequently-updated internal knowledge base. Which is the better fit and why?
options: Fine-tuning, because it always beats RAG
RAG, because it grounds answers in current, retrievable sources and updates without retraining
Neither; put the whole knowledge base in every prompt permanently
Fine-tuning, to avoid ever citing sources
rationale: RAG suits large, changing knowledge because content is retrieved at query time and can be updated without retraining, and answers can cite sources. Stuffing everything into every prompt is infeasible and costly.
referenceText: Retrieval augmented generation guidance

stem: Which are sound architectural practices for a production RAG system on Claude? (Select TWO.)
options: Chunk and index sources, retrieve the most relevant context per query, and pass it with the question
Cache large stable instructions/context with prompt caching to cut cost and latency
Send the entire corpus in every request
Disable evaluation to ship faster
rationale: Effective RAG retrieves only relevant chunks per query; prompt caching reduces cost/latency for stable prefixes. Sending the whole corpus every time and skipping evals are anti-patterns.
referenceText: Prompt caching; RAG patterns

stem: A complex analytical task needs the strongest reasoning available and can tolerate higher latency. The architect should:
options: Use the smallest model and raise temperature
Select the most capable model and consider enabling extended thinking for the hardest steps
Avoid the API and answer from training data
Set max_tokens to 1
rationale: Match the model to task difficulty: the most capable model, optionally with extended thinking, fits hard reasoning tasks where latency is acceptable.
referenceText: Models overview; extended thinking

stem: For a long-context workload with a large, stable system prompt reused across many calls, which technique most reduces cost and latency?
options: Prompt caching of the stable prefix
Increasing temperature
Sending fewer messages by dropping needed context
Switching off streaming
rationale: Prompt caching reuses the stable prefix across requests, cutting cost and latency without dropping needed context.
referenceText: Prompt caching

stem: A high-volume, latency-tolerant nightly job scores millions of records. Which integration pattern is most cost-effective?
options: Synchronous real-time calls one at a time
The Message Batches API for asynchronous bulk processing at reduced cost
Streaming each request to a browser
Fine-tuning a model per record
rationale: Large asynchronous workloads that tolerate delay are the ideal use of the Message Batches API, which processes them at reduced cost.
referenceText: Message Batches API

stem: An enterprise wants to run Claude within its existing AWS data boundary. Which integration option supports this?
options: Claude is only available via the first-party API
Claude models available through Amazon Bedrock (and Google Vertex AI) let teams deploy within their cloud environment
The model must be downloaded and self-hosted on-prem
There is no enterprise deployment option
rationale: Claude is offered through Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI in addition to the first-party API, enabling deployment within a team's existing cloud/governance boundary.
referenceText: Claude on Bedrock / Vertex

stem: Before and after a prompt/model change in production, the most rigorous way to know if quality improved is to:
options: Read a few outputs and decide by feel
Run an evaluation on a representative, versioned test set with graded criteria and compare scores
Ship and wait for complaints
Compare only token costs
rationale: A representative, versioned eval with graded criteria provides objective before/after comparison. Anecdotes, complaint-driven QA, and cost-only comparisons don't measure quality.
referenceText: Develop empirical evaluations

stem: Which is an appropriate use of an LLM-as-judge in an evaluation pipeline?
options: Replacing all human review permanently with no calibration
Scoring outputs against an explicit rubric to scale grading, calibrated against human labels
Grading with no criteria at all
Judging with the same prompt that generated the output, unreviewed
rationale: LLM-as-judge scales grading when driven by an explicit rubric and calibrated against human labels. It should not run rubric-free or entirely uncalibrated.
referenceText: Evaluation — grading methods

stem: For a solution handling regulated personal and health data (e.g., GDPR/HIPAA), which governance practices are appropriate? (Select TWO.)
options: Minimize and control what sensitive data is sent, and choose a deployment/data-handling posture that meets compliance requirements
Maintain human oversight and audit logging for consequential decisions
Send all raw PII to the model by default for convenience
Disable logging entirely so nothing is traceable
rationale: Data minimization, a compliant deployment posture, human oversight, and audit trails are core governance controls for regulated data. Defaulting to sending all PII or removing traceability violates governance.
referenceText: Anthropic trust, safety & compliance

stem: An untrusted-input feature could be targeted by prompt injection to misuse tools. Which mitigation set is most appropriate?
options: Trust all input and grant the agent broad permissions
Delimit and treat untrusted content as data, constrain tool permissions to least privilege, and add guardrails/monitoring
Only increase max_tokens
Rely solely on the model refusing
rationale: Defense-in-depth against injection: isolate untrusted content, apply least-privilege tool permissions, and add guardrails and monitoring. Broad trust or relying solely on refusals is insufficient.
referenceText: Mitigate jailbreaks & prompt injection

stem: When presenting a Claude solution's readiness to non-technical executives, the most effective communication focuses on:
options: Raw benchmark token counts only
Business outcomes, risks and mitigations, evaluation results, and cost/rollout plan in stakeholder-relevant terms
Low-level API parameters
A promise that the system is perfect
rationale: Executive communication should translate technical work into outcomes, risks/mitigations, evidence (evals), and a cost/rollout plan — not raw parameters or overpromises.
referenceText: CCAR-P guide — Stakeholder Communication

stem: Which lifecycle practice best sustains quality after launch as usage and content evolve?
options: Freeze the prompt and never revisit it
Continuously monitor outputs and costs, re-run evals on changes, and iterate with versioned prompts/content
Rely on the initial launch tests indefinitely
Remove monitoring once it works once
rationale: Ongoing monitoring, regression evals on changes, and versioned iteration keep a deployed system healthy as inputs drift. Freezing or dropping monitoring lets quality decay silently.
referenceText: CCAR-P guide — Lifecycle Management

stem: To improve developer productivity and operational enablement across teams adopting Claude, an effective step is to:
options: Let every team reinvent prompts and tooling independently
Provide shared, reusable assets (prompt libraries, evals, MCP servers, CLAUDE.md conventions) and enablement docs
Restrict access so only one person can build
Prohibit evaluation to move faster
rationale: Operational enablement scales through shared reusable assets and documented conventions, reducing duplication and raising baseline quality. Siloing or banning evals hurts productivity and reliability.
referenceText: CCAR-P guide — Developer Productivity & Enablement

stem: A design must degrade gracefully if the model or a tool is temporarily unavailable. Which architectural practice supports this?
options: Assume every call always succeeds
Add timeouts, retries with backoff, fallbacks, and structured error handling around model and tool calls
Remove all error handling to simplify code
Cache nothing and retry infinitely with no limit
rationale: Resilient architectures use timeouts, bounded retries with backoff, fallbacks, and structured error handling. Assuming success or unbounded retries creates fragility and cascading failures.
referenceText: CCAR-P guide — Solution Design & Reliability

stem: An integration must call Claude and several internal systems as tools. Which approach keeps the integration maintainable and standardized?
options: Bespoke one-off glue code per system with no shared contract
Expose internal systems via MCP servers with well-scoped tools/resources the agent can reuse
Embed each system's credentials in the prompt
Fine-tune a model per internal system
rationale: MCP provides a standardized way to expose internal systems as reusable tools/resources, improving maintainability over bespoke glue or credential-in-prompt hacks.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol

stem: Latency in a user-facing feature is too high. Which optimization is most likely to help without lowering answer quality?
options: Prompt caching stable prefixes and right-sizing the model to the task, validated by an eval
Raising temperature
Removing all system instructions
Always using the largest model for every request
rationale: Caching stable prefixes and choosing an appropriately sized model (verified by eval) reduce latency while holding quality. Temperature doesn't reduce latency, stripping instructions harms quality, and always maxing model size raises latency/cost.
referenceText: Reduce latency & cost

stem: A customer-support assistant prepends the same 30,000-token knowledge base and policy document to every request; the prefix is identical across thousands of daily conversations. The architect enables prompt caching. Which cost impact should they communicate to stakeholders?
options: Cache-write tokens cost more than standard input tokens (about 1.25x for the 5-minute TTL), while subsequent cache-read tokens are billed at a small fraction (about 0.1x) of standard input pricing
Cached input tokens are billed identically to uncached input tokens, but request latency drops to zero
After the first request the cached prefix incurs no input-token charge at all, so only output tokens are billed
Caching raises output-token cost by 25% in exchange for storing the prefix indefinitely at no additional charge
rationale: Prompt caching charges a premium to write the cache (roughly 1.25x base input for the default 5-minute TTL) and then bills cache reads at roughly 0.1x base input, which is what drives the savings on a large stable prefix. It never makes cached tokens free (they are read at a reduced rate, not zero), latency is reduced but not to zero, and caching does not increase output-token pricing or store data indefinitely (the default TTL refreshes on use).
referenceText: Prompt caching pricing: cache writes are billed above base input token rates and cache reads at a reduced fraction.

stem: A data team must classify 2 million archived documents with Claude. The job is not time-sensitive and can finish overnight. Which approach best optimizes cost while respecting platform constraints?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly 50% lower cost than standard requests
Send all 2 million requests synchronously in a tight loop and rely on automatic retries to absorb rate-limit errors
Use the standard Messages API with the largest model to finish as fast as possible, since batching costs more per token
Distribute the workload across many API keys to bypass per-account rate limits and lower cost
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large, non-urgent workloads: asynchronous processing within a 24-hour window at about half the per-token cost. A synchronous tight loop wastes cost and hammers rate limits; forcing the largest model synchronously ignores the batch discount; distributing across keys to evade rate limits violates usage policies and does not reduce cost.
referenceText: Message Batches API processes large asynchronous jobs within 24 hours at a 50% discount.

stem: While designing a RAG pipeline, a developer asks which Anthropic endpoint generates the vector embeddings for the document index. What is the most accurate guidance?
options: Anthropic does not offer a first-party embeddings model; the documentation recommends a third-party provider such as Voyage AI to create the vectors
The Messages API returns an embedding vector in its response metadata when embedding_mode is set to true
Claude generates embeddings automatically whenever documents are passed in the request's documents field
The Token Counting endpoint returns embeddings alongside the token count for any submitted text
rationale: Anthropic explicitly states it does not provide its own embedding model and points customers to embedding vendors such as Voyage AI. The Messages API has no embedding_mode, passing documents (for example for citations) does not return vectors, and token counting only returns token counts.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model and recommends providers like Voyage AI.

stem: A RAG system frequently retrieves chunks that cut sentences in half and lose surrounding context, hurting answer quality. Which chunking change most directly addresses this without simply maximizing chunk size?
options: Introduce overlap between adjacent chunks and align boundaries to semantic units such as paragraphs or sentences
Reduce every chunk to a single token so retrieval granularity is maximized
Store the entire corpus as one chunk so context is never split
Disable retrieval entirely and rely solely on the model's parametric knowledge
rationale: Overlapping chunks and boundaries aligned to natural semantic units preserve context across chunk edges without ballooning chunk size. Single-token chunks destroy meaning, one giant chunk defeats retrieval precision and blows the context budget, and disabling retrieval abandons the grounding the system needs.
referenceText: Structuring context by semantic units improves how models use retrieved content.

stem: A regulated-industry client requires that every factual claim in the assistant's answer be traceable to a specific source passage. Which Claude capability most directly supports this?
options: The Citations feature, which has Claude reference the exact source-document passages that support each part of its response
Extended thinking, which exposes the model's internal reasoning as the source of each claim
Prompt caching, which stores the source documents so they can be audited later
The Token Counting endpoint, which maps output tokens back to input passages
rationale: The Citations feature grounds responses in provided documents and returns references to the specific supporting passages, giving verifiable provenance. Extended thinking exposes reasoning, not source attribution; prompt caching only reduces cost/latency of resending documents; token counting does not attribute claims to sources.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude ground its answers in provided documents and cite the exact supporting passages.

stem: Before building an evaluation suite for a summarization feature, the team wants to follow Anthropic's recommended first step. What should they do first?
options: Define specific, measurable success criteria (for example target accuracy, latency, and cost) that the system must meet
Choose the largest available model so evaluation always passes
Ship to production and let user complaints define the quality bar
Write the final production prompt before deciding what 'good' means
rationale: Anthropic's guidance is to define clear, measurable success criteria before building evals, so tests actually measure what matters. Picking the biggest model, shipping first, or finalizing the prompt before defining success all put implementation ahead of the definition of quality.
referenceText: Establish measurable success criteria before designing evaluations.

stem: An architect proposes an LLM-as-judge (model-graded) evaluation for open-ended responses. Which practices improve the reliability of this approach? (Select 2.)
options: Provide the judge model with a detailed rubric and concrete grading criteria
Ask the judge to return a structured verdict (such as a numeric score or pass/fail) that can be aggregated
Reuse the same single test example for every run to keep results stable
Remove any reference/expected answer so the judge cannot be biased by it
Grade primarily on response length, since longer answers are generally better
rationale: Model-graded evals are far more reliable when the judge is given an explicit rubric and asked to emit a structured, aggregatable verdict. Using one example gives no coverage, withholding a reference answer removes useful grounding rather than reducing bias, and length is not a valid quality proxy.
referenceText: Model-graded evaluations should use clear rubrics and structured, machine-readable outputs.

stem: A production incident requires tracing one specific failed Claude request when contacting support and correlating internal logs. Which value is most useful to capture per request in the observability pipeline?
options: The unique request ID returned in the API response headers
The client machine's local wall-clock timestamp only
The developer console's color theme setting
The number of retries performed by the CDN edge
rationale: Each API response includes a unique request ID that Anthropic support and your own logs use to identify a specific call; logging it is the key to traceability. A local timestamp alone is ambiguous across many concurrent requests, and console theme or CDN retry counts have no diagnostic value for a specific request.
referenceText: API responses include a request-id used to identify and troubleshoot individual requests.

stem: To make a Claude API integration resilient, which HTTP status codes should trigger an automatic retry with exponential backoff rather than an immediate hard failure? (Select 2.)
options: 429 (rate limit exceeded)
529 (API temporarily overloaded)
400 (invalid request / malformed input)
401 (authentication error)
403 (permission denied)
rationale: 429 (rate limited) and 529 (overloaded) are transient conditions that typically succeed on a later attempt, so backoff-and-retry is appropriate. 400, 401, and 403 are deterministic client errors (bad request, bad credentials, insufficient permission) that will fail identically on retry and should surface immediately.
referenceText: 429 and 529 are transient errors suited to retry with backoff; 4xx auth/validation errors are not.

stem: A feature generates long-form reports that can take well over a minute to produce, and non-streaming requests occasionally hit network timeouts. Which design change best mitigates this while keeping the interaction responsive?
options: Enable streaming so tokens are delivered incrementally, avoiding a single long-blocking response and reducing timeout risk
Increase max_tokens so the response finishes faster
Disable retries so the request fails immediately instead of timing out
Force every long request onto a smaller-context model regardless of quality needs
rationale: Streaming returns partial output as it is generated, so the connection stays active and long generations do not trip idle/network timeouts; Anthropic recommends streaming for long-running requests. Raising max_tokens can lengthen (not shorten) generation, disabling retries removes resilience, and downgrading the model sacrifices quality without solving the timeout mechanism.
referenceText: Use streaming for long-running requests to avoid timeouts on non-streaming calls.

stem: An architect wants the assistant to stay available when a specific Claude model in one region is temporarily overloaded (HTTP 529). Which resilience pattern best addresses this?
options: Retry with backoff and, if overload persists, fail over to an alternate region or a comparable model
Cache the previous answer and always return it during any outage regardless of the new question
Increase the request timeout to several hours so the call eventually succeeds
Remove all error handling so failures surface to end users immediately
rationale: 529 signals transient overload, so combining bounded retry-with-backoff with a fallback to another region or comparable model preserves availability. Returning a stale cached answer to a different question is incorrect, multi-hour timeouts degrade UX and tie up resources, and removing error handling makes the system less resilient.
referenceText: Handle 529 overloaded responses with retries and fallback routing.

stem: A healthcare app must send clinical notes to Claude but wants to limit exposure of directly identifying data. Applying the principle of data minimization, which design choice is most appropriate?
options: Redact or tokenize direct identifiers (names, MRNs, SSNs) before sending, transmitting only the minimum data needed for the task
Send the full unredacted record so the model has maximum context for accuracy
Embed identifiers in the system prompt, where they are assumed to be private
Log every complete raw payload indefinitely for future debugging
rationale: Data minimization means sending only the data required and stripping or tokenizing direct identifiers before transmission. Sending full records maximizes exposure, the system prompt is not a privacy boundary, and indefinite raw-payload logging directly contradicts minimization and retention limits.
referenceText: Minimize and de-identify data sent to and retained by the system.

stem: A covered entity plans to process protected health information (PHI) through the Claude API. What is the appropriate first governance step with Anthropic?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and configure the account per the agreed data-handling terms before sending PHI
Rely on the default consumer terms, which automatically cover PHI for every account
Send PHI only through the developer console UI, which is exempt from HIPAA
Encrypt the API key, which by itself satisfies HIPAA for PHI processing
rationale: HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement between the covered entity and Anthropic before PHI is processed, along with the corresponding account configuration. Default consumer terms do not cover PHI, the console UI is not exempt from HIPAA, and encrypting a key is unrelated to the BAA and data-handling requirements.
referenceText: Processing PHI requires a BAA and appropriate data-handling configuration with Anthropic.

stem: An EU customer subject to GDPR asks how to reduce retention of personal data submitted to the Claude API and support data-subject deletion requests. Which architectural measures align best? (Select 2.)
options: Minimize and pseudonymize personal data before submission, keeping the identity mapping under the customer's control
Explore zero-data-retention / no-training data-handling options and set retention to the minimum needed
Store every prompt and completion permanently to guarantee an immutable record for all users
Assume GDPR does not apply because the API is operated from the United States
Disable all logging entirely, including the security audit logs required for compliance
rationale: Pseudonymizing before submission (with the customer holding the mapping) and configuring minimal or zero data retention both reduce personal-data exposure and ease deletion. Permanent storage conflicts with data-subject rights, GDPR applies based on the data subjects not server location, and disabling all logging (including required audit logs) undermines rather than supports compliance.
referenceText: Reduce personal-data retention and pseudonymize inputs to support GDPR obligations.

stem: An enterprise has standardized on AWS with IAM, VPC networking, and centralized billing, and wants to use Claude without managing a separate Anthropic account. Which deployment option fits best?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, reusing existing AWS IAM, networking, and consolidated billing
Only the first-party Anthropic API can serve enterprise workloads; Bedrock is for prototypes only
Deploy the Claude model weights directly onto customer-managed EC2 instances
Use Bedrock purely as a proxy that forwards to the Anthropic API with the customer's Anthropic key
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets the enterprise consume Claude through AWS-native IAM, VPC, and billing, matching their standardization. Bedrock is a fully production-grade path, Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting on EC2, and Bedrock is a first-class provider, not a proxy to the Anthropic API.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock provides access via AWS IAM, networking, and billing.

stem: A team deploying Claude on Google Vertex AI gets model-not-found (404) errors when calling a model in a particular region. What is the most likely architectural cause?
options: The requested Claude model is not available/enabled in that specific Vertex AI region, so requests must target a region where the model is offered
Vertex AI never supports Claude models, so the team must switch providers
Claude on Vertex AI additionally requires an Anthropic API key alongside Google credentials
The model is deprecated globally and cannot be called from any region
rationale: On Vertex AI, Claude models are offered in specific regions and must be enabled there; calling a region where the model is not available yields model-not-found. Vertex AI does support Claude, it uses Google Cloud credentials (not an Anthropic key), and a region-scoped 404 points to regional availability rather than global deprecation.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI is region-specific; requests must target a region where the model is available.

stem: A solution uses the Model Context Protocol to connect Claude to internal systems. Which description of MCP's architecture is accurate?
options: MCP is a client-server protocol in which host applications run MCP clients that connect to MCP servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts
MCP is a proprietary Anthropic-only binary format that only Claude models can parse
MCP replaces application code by executing all tool logic inside the model weights
MCP is strictly a database-migration standard unrelated to model tool integration
rationale: MCP defines an open client-server architecture: a host runs MCP clients that connect to MCP servers, which expose tools, resources, and prompts. It is an open standard (not Anthropic-only or a closed binary format), it does not run logic inside model weights, and it is unrelated to database migrations.
referenceText: MCP uses a client-server architecture where servers expose tools, resources, and prompts to host applications.

stem: A pipeline runs a high-volume, latency-sensitive intent-classification step before a complex multi-step reasoning step. Applying model right-sizing to optimize cost and latency, how should the architect assign models?
options: Use a smaller, faster model (for example Claude Haiku) for the simple high-volume classification and reserve a larger model (Sonnet or Opus) for the complex reasoning step
Use the largest model for every step to keep the architecture uniform and simple
Use the smallest model for every step regardless of task complexity to minimize cost
Alternate models randomly per request to spread load across model tiers
rationale: Right-sizing matches model capability to task difficulty: a fast, inexpensive model handles the simple high-volume step while a more capable model handles the hard reasoning step, optimizing both cost and latency. Using the largest model everywhere wastes cost/latency, the smallest everywhere sacrifices quality on the hard step, and random assignment yields unpredictable quality.
referenceText: Select the smallest model that meets each task's requirements and reserve larger models for harder tasks.

stem: Anthropic announces a retirement date for a model version the client's production system depends on. Which lifecycle and stakeholder-communication action is most appropriate?
options: Proactively plan and test migration to a supported successor model against the existing eval suite before the retirement date, and communicate the timeline to stakeholders
Wait until the model is fully retired and requests start failing, then react
Pin the deprecated model indefinitely and assume it will keep working
Silently swap the model in production without re-running any evaluations
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means migrating to a supported model ahead of the retirement date, validating it against your evals, and keeping stakeholders informed of the plan and timeline. Waiting for failures, pinning a retired model, or swapping without re-evaluating all risk outages or silent quality regressions.
referenceText: Plan and test migrations to successor models before deprecated models are retired.

stem: A compliance team requires an audit trail of who invoked the AI assistant, when, and what category of action was taken. Where should this audit logging be implemented in the architecture?
options: In the application/orchestration layer the customer controls, capturing actor, timestamp, request metadata, and outcome for each invocation
Inside the model weights, which automatically retain a per-user audit log
Nowhere, because the response's stop_reason field already constitutes a complete audit trail
Only in the end user's browser local storage
rationale: Audit logging is a customer responsibility implemented in the application/orchestration layer, where actor identity, timestamps, request metadata, and outcomes are known and controllable. Model weights do not store audit logs, stop_reason only explains why generation ended, and browser local storage is neither authoritative nor tamper-resistant.
referenceText: Audit logging of invocations is implemented in the customer-controlled application layer.

stem: A team builds RAG over long technical manuals. Retrieval uses fixed 256-token chunks, and Claude's answers frequently omit steps because multi-step procedures and tables are split across chunk boundaries. Which change most directly improves answer completeness?
options: Increase the embedding model's output dimensionality to capture more meaning per vector
Adopt larger, structure-aware chunks with overlap that keep procedures, tables, and section boundaries intact
Replace dense retrieval with keyword-only BM25 search to guarantee exact matches
Stop chunking entirely and embed each full manual as a single vector
rationale: Structure-aware chunking with overlap keeps semantically coupled content (procedures, tables) together so retrieved passages are self-contained, directly fixing the split-context problem. Higher embedding dimensionality does not stop content from being split. BM25 alone loses semantic recall and does not preserve structure. Embedding whole manuals produces one coarse vector that destroys retrieval precision and can exceed practical limits.
referenceText: Long context tips: preserve document structure and use meaningful, overlapping chunk boundaries so related content stays together for retrieval.

stem: An architect needs verifiable, character-level source attributions in a RAG assistant so that each claim links back to the exact supporting text in the provided documents. What is the most robust way to implement this on the Claude API?
options: Instruct Claude in the system prompt to append a bracketed source number after every sentence
Pass sources as document content blocks and enable the Citations feature so Claude returns grounded citation objects
Ask Claude to output the full source text alongside every answer for manual verification
Lower temperature to 0 so Claude never invents citations
rationale: The Citations feature lets you supply documents as content blocks and returns structured citation objects pointing to the exact supporting spans, which is auditable and resistant to fabrication. Prompt-only citation formatting is unverified and easily hallucinated. Echoing full source text is wasteful and still not a verified mapping. Temperature 0 reduces randomness but does not produce grounded, structured references.
referenceText: Citations returns fine-grained references to the source documents you provide, rather than relying on the model to format citations itself.

stem: During RAG design a team asks which Anthropic endpoint to call to generate the vector embeddings for their document index. What is the correct architectural guidance?
options: Call the Messages API with a special embeddings flag to return vectors
The Claude API does not provide a first-party embeddings endpoint; use a recommended third-party embeddings provider such as Voyage AI
Embeddings are produced automatically as a side output of every completion request
Use the token-counting endpoint, whose token IDs serve as embeddings
rationale: Anthropic does not ship its own embeddings model and recommends third-party providers (e.g., Voyage AI) for embeddings. The Messages API returns text/tool outputs, not embedding vectors. Completions do not emit embeddings as a side effect. Token IDs from token counting are not semantic embeddings.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model; the embeddings guide recommends third-party providers such as Voyage AI.

stem: A chat product resends a 40,000-token policy handbook and tool schema unchanged on every turn, driving high input-token cost and latency. Which content is the best candidate for prompt caching, and why?
options: The user's latest message, because it is what changes each turn
The large, stable handbook and tool definitions placed before the variable conversation, because cached prefixes are reused across requests
The model's streamed output tokens, because output dominates cost
Nothing, because caching only helps when the entire request is identical each time
rationale: Prompt caching reuses a stable prefix across requests, so the large unchanging handbook and tool schema (placed before variable content) yield the biggest savings. The user's per-turn message changes and cannot be reused. Output tokens are not cacheable input. Caching benefits stable prefixes even when the suffix (user turn) differs, so 'entirely identical' is false.
referenceText: Prompt caching stores and reuses stable prefix content across requests; put large, static context up front and mark cache breakpoints.

stem: An engineer wants to maximize prompt-cache hit rate across many similar requests. How should the prompt be structured?
options: Interleave dynamic user variables throughout the static instructions so the model sees them in context
Order content from most static to most dynamic, placing cache breakpoints after the stable prefix and keeping variable content last
Put the changing user question first so it is prioritized by the cache
Randomize block order per request to avoid stale cache entries
rationale: Caching keys on an identical prefix, so putting static content first with a breakpoint after it and variable content last maximizes reuse. Interleaving dynamic values breaks the shared prefix. Leading with the changing question prevents any stable prefix from forming. Randomizing order defeats caching entirely.
referenceText: Structure prompts with static content first and dynamic content last so the cacheable prefix is stable across requests.

stem: A nightly job scores 2 million archived reviews for sentiment. Results are not needed until the next morning, and cost is the primary concern. Which approach best fits the requirements?
options: Fan out 2 million individual synchronous Messages requests behind a high concurrency limit
Submit the work through the Message Batches API to process asynchronously at a reduced per-token cost within the batch window
Stream each request to reduce time-to-first-token across the job
Increase max_tokens so more reviews fit in a single request
rationale: The Message Batches API is built for large, latency-tolerant workloads, processing asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly half the cost, which matches an overnight, cost-sensitive job. Millions of synchronous calls are costlier and rate-limit constrained. Streaming optimizes perceived latency, irrelevant here. max_tokens governs output length, not batching multiple reviews.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a discount, ideal for non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: A pipeline classifies millions of short support tickets into 12 categories; a smaller model already meets accuracy for the bulk, but a minority of tickets need nuanced multi-step reasoning. Which two choices best balance cost and quality? (Select 2.)
options: Route all traffic to the most capable model to guarantee uniform quality
Use a smaller, faster model (e.g., Haiku) for the high-volume, well-handled classifications
Escalate only ambiguous or low-confidence tickets to a more capable model (e.g., Sonnet or Opus)
Disable streaming everywhere to reduce token usage
Always request the maximum max_tokens to avoid any truncation
rationale: Right-sizing means serving the bulk with a cheaper model and escalating only the hard cases to a stronger model, capturing most of the savings while protecting quality where it matters. Sending everything to the top model wastes cost. Streaming does not change token counts. Maxing out max_tokens raises cost and latency without improving classification.
referenceText: Choosing a model: match model capability to task difficulty and use tiered routing to balance cost, speed, and quality.

stem: A production client intermittently receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses. What is the correct resilience pattern for these transient errors?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when present
Treat both as permanent failures and surface the error to the end user immediately
Lower the request's max_tokens and resend without any delay
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; exponential backoff with jitter (and respecting retry-after) spreads load and recovers gracefully. Tight-loop retries amplify congestion and worsen the problem. These are retryable, not permanent. Reducing max_tokens does not address rate limiting or overload and immediate resends still hammer the service.
referenceText: Errors: 429 and 529 are transient; clients should retry with exponential backoff and respect retry-after guidance.

stem: You are hardening a customer-facing Claude application against transient capacity errors without degrading user experience. Which two design measures are appropriate? (Select 2.)
options: Retry immediately and indefinitely with no delay until a response arrives
Apply exponential backoff with jitter and cap the number of retries
Provide a fallback path such as a secondary region, service tier, or model when retries are exhausted
Return the raw upstream error to every user and cache it for five minutes
Set the client timeout to infinite so no request is ever cancelled
rationale: Bounded backoff with jitter plus a fallback path (secondary region/tier/model) preserves availability and UX during transient overload. Infinite immediate retries cause retry storms. Broadcasting and caching errors degrades everyone. Infinite timeouts let requests hang, exhausting resources instead of failing fast to a fallback.
referenceText: Service tiers and capacity management support fallback strategies for handling load and transient errors gracefully.

stem: An architect is explaining the Model Context Protocol (MCP) topology to stakeholders. Which description correctly characterizes MCP's architecture?
options: A single monolithic server that embeds the model, the application, and all data sources in one process
A client-server model where a host application runs MCP clients that connect over a transport to MCP servers exposing tools, resources, and prompts
A peer-to-peer mesh in which each model instance directly queries every other model's memory
A browser-only protocol that requires all context to be passed as URL query parameters
rationale: MCP defines a host application containing MCP clients that connect via a transport to MCP servers, which expose capabilities like tools, resources, and prompts. It is not a monolith embedding the model. It is not a model-to-model mesh. It is transport-agnostic (e.g., stdio, HTTP), not URL-parameter based.
referenceText: MCP architecture: hosts run clients that connect to servers over a transport; servers expose tools, resources, and prompts.

stem: In an MCP server design, the team must decide whether to expose a capability as a tool or as a resource. Which distinction should guide the decision?
options: Tools and resources are interchangeable; choose whichever name reads better
Tools are model-invoked actions that can have side effects, while resources are application/user-controlled context that the host exposes to the model
Resources are the only way to let the model take actions, and tools are read-only
Tools may only return text, whereas resources may only return images
rationale: MCP tools are model-controlled, action-oriented capabilities (often with side effects) the model can invoke, while resources are application-controlled, data/context exposed to the model. They are not interchangeable. The action/read-only mapping is reversed in the third option. Neither tools nor resources are restricted to a single content type.
referenceText: Resources are application-controlled context exposed to the model, distinct from tools, which are model-controlled actions.

stem: An enterprise standardized on AWS wants to run Claude while keeping inference within their AWS security and networking boundary and reusing existing IAM controls. Which deployment is the best fit?
options: Call the first-party Anthropic API directly from AWS Lambda using an Anthropic API key
Use Claude on Amazon Bedrock, invoking models via Bedrock APIs governed by AWS IAM within the account's region
Proxy all requests through a self-hosted copy of the model weights on EC2
Route traffic through Google Vertex AI to reach Claude from AWS
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock runs within the customer's AWS account and region, using IAM for auth and AWS-native controls, satisfying the boundary and governance requirements. The first-party API sits outside AWS's IAM/networking boundary. Anthropic does not distribute self-hostable weights. Vertex AI is Google Cloud, not AWS.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets you invoke Claude through Bedrock using AWS IAM and regional controls within your AWS account.

stem: A Google Cloud customer must call Claude while enforcing data residency in a specific European region and using their existing GCP identity controls. Which detail is essential to the design?
options: Vertex AI has a single global endpoint, so region cannot be controlled
Claude on Vertex AI is invoked through regional endpoints authenticated with GCP IAM, letting you pin requests to a chosen region
You must export an Anthropic API key into GCP because Vertex does not manage Claude auth
Vertex AI requires downloading Claude weights into the project bucket before use
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses regional endpoints and GCP IAM, so requests can be pinned to a specific region for residency and governed by native identity controls. Vertex is regional, not single-global. Auth is via GCP IAM, not an Anthropic key. No weight download is involved.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI is accessed via regional endpoints and authenticated through Google Cloud IAM.

stem: Before building a summarization feature, an architect wants a defensible way to know whether a candidate prompt/model is good enough for release. What should the evaluation design include first?
options: A single hand-picked prompt that looks impressive in a demo
A representative, versioned test set with explicit success criteria and graders (code-based, LLM-based, or human) run consistently across changes
Only production A/B traffic, with no offline test set
A rule to ship whenever the model's output subjectively feels acceptable to the developer
rationale: A representative, versioned eval set tied to explicit success criteria and consistent graders enables objective, repeatable release decisions and regression detection. A single demo prompt is not representative. Relying solely on live A/B lacks a pre-release gate and controlled comparison. Subjective feel is not measurable or reproducible.
referenceText: Develop tests: build representative eval sets with clear success criteria and graders to measure quality across changes.

stem: To support production observability and efficient Anthropic support escalations, which per-request metadata is most important to capture in application logs?
options: Only the final rendered answer shown to the user
The response request_id together with token usage, latency, model, and stop_reason
The user's raw password and full session cookie for correlation
Nothing, since Anthropic retains full request logs on the customer's behalf
rationale: Logging the request_id plus usage, latency, model, and stop_reason gives traceability, cost/performance monitoring, and a handle Anthropic support can use to investigate specific calls. Storing only the answer loses diagnostic context. Logging credentials is a security violation. Customers should not assume Anthropic retains their request logs for them.
referenceText: API responses include a request_id used to identify and troubleshoot individual requests with support.

stem: An EU-based application processes sensitive customer data through Claude and must uphold GDPR data-minimization and residency expectations. Which two design measures directly support this? (Select 2.)
options: Send each customer's complete record on every request so the model always has full context
Redact or omit personal data that is not required for the task before sending it to the model
Deploy through a regional endpoint (e.g., Bedrock or Vertex in an EU region) to keep processing in-region
Persist all raw prompts and completions indefinitely for future analytics
Embed customers' national ID numbers into the system prompt to personalize responses
rationale: Minimizing the personal data sent (redaction/omission) and pinning processing to an EU regional endpoint align with data-minimization and residency principles. Sending full records and embedding national IDs maximize rather than minimize exposure. Indefinite retention of raw data conflicts with storage-limitation and minimization goals.
referenceText: Regional endpoints on Vertex AI let you control where requests are processed, supporting data residency requirements.

stem: A US healthcare provider intends to process protected health information (PHI) with Claude. Which architectural prerequisite must be satisfied for a compliant design?
options: Any Anthropic API key automatically covers HIPAA, so no agreement is required
PHI processing requires a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) and an appropriately configured, eligible deployment such as Claude on Amazon Bedrock under the AWS BAA
HIPAA compliance is achieved simply by setting temperature to 0
Storing PHI in the prompt is fine as long as responses are streamed
rationale: Handling PHI requires a signed BAA and a HIPAA-eligible deployment configuration (e.g., Claude on Amazon Bedrock covered under the AWS BAA). A default API key does not confer HIPAA coverage. Temperature and streaming are unrelated to regulatory eligibility.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock can be used within AWS's HIPAA-eligible services under a Business Associate Agreement.

stem: A US federal agency requires a FedRAMP-authorized environment for a Claude workload. Which deployment approach is the appropriate architectural choice?
options: Call the public first-party Anthropic API and self-attest FedRAMP compliance
Deploy Claude on Amazon Bedrock within AWS GovCloud / FedRAMP-authorized boundaries that meet the required authorization level
Run the workload from any commercial cloud region, since FedRAMP applies only to storage
Skip FedRAMP because encryption in transit is sufficient for federal use
rationale: Meeting FedRAMP means running within an authorized boundary, and Claude on Amazon Bedrock in AWS GovCloud/FedRAMP-authorized environments provides that inheritance. Self-attesting on the public API does not confer authorization. FedRAMP governs the whole service, not just storage, and TLS alone does not satisfy it.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock is available within AWS environments that carry FedRAMP authorizations, including GovCloud.

stem: A regulated enterprise running Claude on Amazon Bedrock must demonstrate an auditable trail of model usage. Which two measures provide traceability and audit evidence? (Select 2.)
options: Enable AWS CloudTrail to record Bedrock API invocations
Persist the request_id returned with each response alongside application audit logs
Disable all logging to reduce storage cost and simplify compliance
Rely on the model to recall prior requests when auditors ask
Store only the final user-visible answer with no request metadata
rationale: CloudTrail captures Bedrock invocation events and persisting the request_id with app logs links each interaction to a traceable identifier, together forming an audit trail. Disabling logging destroys evidence. The model is stateless across requests and cannot serve as an audit source. Answer-only storage omits the metadata auditors need.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock integrates with AWS logging services such as CloudTrail for auditing model invocations.

stem: An architect is setting expectations with business stakeholders for the lifecycle of a Claude-powered feature. Which practice best supports maintainability and clear communication over time?
options: Define success criteria and an evaluation suite up front, version prompts and models, and monitor quality against those criteria after launch
Freeze the initial prompt permanently and avoid re-evaluating once the feature ships
Rely on stakeholder anecdotes as the sole signal of ongoing quality
Upgrade to every new model the day it releases without re-running evaluations
rationale: Defining measurable success criteria and evals, versioning prompts/models, and monitoring against those criteria gives stakeholders a shared, objective view across the lifecycle and catches regression or drift. Freezing prompts ignores changing data and requirements. Anecdotes are not reliable metrics. Blind model upgrades without re-evaluation risk silent regressions.
referenceText: Define success: establish measurable success criteria and evaluations to guide development and ongoing monitoring.

stem: You are architecting a document-analysis service that sends the same 40-page policy manual plus a large tool-definition block to Claude on every request, followed by a short, per-user question. You enable prompt caching to cut cost and latency. How should the request be structured so the cache is actually reused across users?
options: Place the stable manual and tool definitions at the very beginning of the request and set the cache_control breakpoint on the last stable block, keeping the variable user question after it
Put the per-user question first with its own cache_control breakpoint so each user's turn is cached independently
Interleave the manual content with each user question so every block gets its own cache entry
Set cache_control on every content block in the request to maximize the number of cache hits
rationale: Prompt caching matches on the longest common prefix, so all static, reusable content (system prompt, tool definitions, the manual) must come first, with the cache breakpoint marking the end of the stable prefix; the variable user question goes after it and is never cached. Putting the question first breaks the shared prefix so nothing is reused. Interleaving destroys the contiguous prefix. Caching every block does not help because the cache still keys on prefix position and adds needless write overhead. Reference: Prompt caching docs.
referenceText: Anthropic — Prompt caching (cache breakpoints and prefix matching)

stem: A team needs to run Claude over 2 million historical support tickets to generate summaries for an internal knowledge base. The job has no real-time requirement and must minimize cost. Which approach best fits this workload?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes large asynchronous jobs within 24 hours at a reduced token cost
Send the requests through the standard streaming Messages API to get partial results as fast as possible
Fan the requests out across many concurrent standard synchronous calls and rely on rate-limit retries
Use the Token Counting endpoint to process the tickets in a single aggregated request
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads: requests are processed asynchronously (target within 24 hours) at roughly half the cost of standard calls, which directly matches a bulk offline summarization job. Streaming optimizes for perceived latency, not cost or throughput. Blasting synchronous calls fights rate limits and costs full price. Token Counting only estimates token usage and does not generate summaries. Reference: Message Batches / batch processing docs.
referenceText: Anthropic — Batch processing (Message Batches API)

stem: A regulated financial RAG application must show, for every claim in Claude's answer, exactly which passage of the retrieved source documents supports it, so compliance can audit responses. Which built-in capability best satisfies this requirement?
options: Pass the retrieved sources as document content blocks and enable the Citations feature so Claude returns claims linked to the specific supporting spans
Lower the temperature to 0 so the model deterministically repeats the source text verbatim
Add a system-prompt instruction telling Claude to 'always be accurate and cite sources' and parse the free text afterward
Increase the retrieval top-k so more documents are in context, guaranteeing traceability
rationale: The Citations feature lets you supply sources as document blocks and returns structured citations that tie each generated statement back to the exact supporting passages, which is precisely what auditable grounding requires. Temperature 0 improves determinism but provides no source linkage. A prose instruction yields unstructured, unreliable citations you must brittle-parse. Raising top-k adds context but produces no per-claim traceability. Reference: Citations docs.
referenceText: Anthropic — Citations

stem: A high-throughput pipeline must classify each inbound email into one of 12 routing categories. The classification is simple, but volume is millions per day and each call must return in a few hundred milliseconds under a tight per-token budget. Which model-selection strategy is most appropriate?
options: Right-size to the fastest, lowest-cost model (a Haiku-class model) since the task is well-scoped and latency- and cost-sensitive
Default to the most capable Opus-class model for every request to guarantee the highest possible accuracy
Route each email to Opus first and fall back to Haiku only when Opus times out
Randomly alternate between models on each request to balance cost and quality
rationale: Model right-sizing means matching model capability to task complexity: a narrow, high-volume classification with strict latency and cost targets is the canonical fit for the smallest, fastest Haiku-class model. Always using Opus wastes cost and latency on an easy task. Trying Opus first then falling back inverts the priority and adds latency on the common path. Random alternation makes cost and quality unpredictable and untestable. Reference: Models overview / choosing a model.
referenceText: Anthropic — Models overview (choosing the right model)

stem: You are designing a Claude-based intake assistant for a US healthcare provider that will process protected health information (PHI). Which design decisions most directly support HIPAA-aligned handling of that data? (Select 2.)
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and use an offering configured for PHI, applying data-minimization so only the PHI necessary for the task is sent
Scrub or avoid PHI in application logs, traces, and error messages, and enable audit logging of access to the system
Embed patient records into the model's system prompt permanently so future requests never need to resend PHI
Disable all retries and fallbacks so PHI is never transmitted more than once under any circumstance
Rely solely on TLS in transit and assume no further contractual or configuration controls are required
rationale: HIPAA compliance for a covered entity's vendor requires a BAA plus a configuration approved for PHI, combined with data minimization (send only what the task needs). Keeping PHI out of logs/traces and enabling audit logging address the confidentiality and accountability requirements. Models do not permanently 'learn' data placed in a system prompt, so that option is technically false and would just resend PHI anyway. Disabling retries harms resilience without any privacy benefit. TLS alone ignores the required contractual and operational controls. Reference: HIPAA/compliance and usage policy guidance.
referenceText: Anthropic — Trust & compliance (HIPAA / BAA) and data handling

stem: An enterprise already runs its workloads in Google Cloud, requires that model inference stay within a specific region for data-residency reasons, and wants to inherit its existing cloud IAM and VPC controls rather than manage a separate API key. Which deployment choice best fits?
options: Access Claude through Google Vertex AI, using the customer's GCP project, region, and IAM/VPC controls for governance
Call the first-party Anthropic API directly and add a client-side region check before each request
Deploy on Amazon Bedrock so the workload can stay inside the existing Google Cloud environment
Self-host the Claude model weights inside the customer's GCP project to control the region
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI lets a GCP-native customer run inference within their chosen project and region and govern access through native GCP IAM and networking, satisfying data-residency and existing-control requirements. The first-party API does not inherit GCP IAM/VPC and a client-side check does not enforce where inference runs. Bedrock is AWS, not Google Cloud. Claude weights are not distributed for customer self-hosting. Reference: Claude on Vertex AI docs.
referenceText: Anthropic — Claude on Google Vertex AI

stem: Your production service occasionally receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses from the API during traffic spikes. Which client-side strategy is the most resilient way to handle these?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring any retry-after header, and cap the number of attempts before surfacing a graceful failure
Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as permanent failures and drop the request without retrying
Switch to a larger model on every 429 or 529 because a bigger model has higher limits
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient conditions; the resilient pattern is bounded exponential backoff with jitter, respecting retry-after to avoid thundering-herd retries, and eventually degrading gracefully if attempts are exhausted. Immediate tight-loop retries amplify the overload. Dropping transient errors needlessly loses recoverable requests. Model size does not raise rate limits and adds cost/latency. Reference: API errors and rate-limit handling docs.
referenceText: Anthropic — Errors and rate limits (retry guidance)

stem: An architect wants Claude-based agents to reach internal systems (a ticketing database, a wiki, and a filesystem) through a consistent, reusable integration layer instead of bespoke glue code for each data source. Which statement best describes what the Model Context Protocol (MCP) provides for this design?
options: An open standard in which MCP servers expose tools and resources that any MCP-compatible client can consume, decoupling integrations from any single application
A proprietary Anthropic-only wire format that replaces the Messages API for all model calls
A model fine-tuning technique that bakes each internal system's data directly into the model weights
A managed vector database that automatically embeds and retrieves the internal systems' content
rationale: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context: servers expose tools, resources, and prompts, and any MCP-compatible client (host) can connect to them, so integrations are written once and reused rather than rebuilt per application. It is not a replacement for the Messages API, not a fine-tuning method, and not a vector store. Reference: Anthropic MCP docs.
referenceText: Anthropic — Model Context Protocol (MCP)

stem: You are designing the retrieval layer of a RAG system over a large corpus of long technical manuals. Which chunking-and-retrieval design best balances retrieval precision with preserving enough context for accurate answers?
options: Split documents into semantically coherent chunks with modest overlap, embed each chunk, retrieve the top-k most similar chunks for a query, and pass them to Claude with source metadata for citations
Embed each entire manual as a single vector and always retrieve the whole document into the context window
Chunk strictly on a fixed character count with no overlap and no regard for sentence or section boundaries
Skip embeddings entirely and rely on the model's training knowledge to answer corpus-specific questions
rationale: Effective RAG uses semantically aware chunks sized for the embedding model with some overlap so boundary context is not lost, then retrieves top-k by similarity and passes the chunks with metadata to enable grounded, citable answers. Embedding whole manuals gives coarse vectors and blows the context budget. Rigid fixed-length no-overlap splitting fractures meaning across boundaries and hurts retrieval quality. Skipping retrieval defeats the purpose of RAG and invites hallucination on corpus-specific facts. Reference: embeddings and RAG guidance.
referenceText: Anthropic — Embeddings and retrieval-augmented generation

stem: Before promoting a Claude-powered feature to production, the team wants a repeatable way to measure quality and catch regressions when prompts or models change. Which evaluation approach is the soundest foundation?
options: Build a version-controlled, representative held-out test set with defined grading criteria, and automate scoring with code-based checks and/or an LLM-as-judge grader run on every change
Have engineers manually spot-check a few outputs each release and approve by consensus
Rely on production user complaints as the primary signal that quality has regressed
Assume that once a prompt looks good in one demo it will generalize, and re-test only if customers escalate
rationale: A durable eval strategy uses a representative, versioned test set with explicit success criteria and automated grading (deterministic checks where possible, an LLM judge for open-ended outputs) so every prompt or model change is measured consistently and regressions are caught before release. Ad-hoc spot checks are not reproducible, waiting for user complaints or escalations is reactive and lets regressions reach users, and one-demo generalization is unreliable. Reference: developing and running evaluations.
referenceText: Anthropic — Create strong empirical evaluations / develop tests

stem: A model version your production application depends on has been announced for deprecation with a retirement date. As the architect, which actions best manage this lifecycle transition while keeping stakeholders informed? (Select 2.)
options: Run your existing evaluation suite against the newer target model to compare quality, latency, and cost, and adjust prompts before cutting over ahead of the retirement date
Communicate the timeline, expected behavior changes, and rollout plan to affected stakeholders and schedule the migration with a rollback path
Ignore the notice until the deprecated model stops responding, then react to the outage
Pin the application permanently to the deprecated model ID so behavior never changes
Immediately switch every workload to the newest model in production with no evaluation, since newer is always better
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means testing the replacement model against your own evals to quantify quality/latency/cost deltas and re-tuning prompts before migrating ahead of the deadline, plus proactively communicating the timeline and change impact to stakeholders with a scheduled, reversible rollout. Waiting for an outage is a guaranteed disruption. A deprecated model ID will eventually be retired, so pinning forever is not viable. Cutting over untested assumes behavior is identical, which prompts and evals exist to verify. Reference: model deprecations and migration guidance.
referenceText: Anthropic — Model deprecations and lifecycle

stem: An architect designs a customer-support assistant that sends the same 40,000-token policy manual and system prompt on every request, followed by a short, varying user question. They enable prompt caching by placing a single cache_control breakpoint at the end of the policy manual. Which statement about how this caching behaves is correct?
options: The cached prefix is reused only when the tokens before and including the breakpoint are byte-for-byte identical to a prior request; any change to the system prompt or manual invalidates the cache from the point of difference onward.
Because the user question changes each turn, the cache never produces a hit, so the breakpoint provides no cost benefit.
Caching applies to the entire request including the varying user question, so the whole prompt must be identical for a hit.
The cached content is stored permanently until manually deleted, so stale policy text will keep being served even after the manual is updated.
rationale: Prompt caching matches on an exact prefix up to and including the cache_control breakpoint; identical static content (system prompt + manual) yields a cache read while the trailing variable question is processed normally, so placing the breakpoint after the stable content is exactly right. The 'never hits' option is wrong: the varying question comes AFTER the breakpoint, so the stable prefix still hits. The 'entire request' option is wrong because only the prefix, not the whole request, must match. The 'permanent' option is wrong: cache entries are ephemeral (default 5-minute TTL, refreshed on use), not permanent, and updating the manual changes the prefix and creates a new cache entry.
referenceText: Prompt caching references cached prefixes; the cache is invalidated at the first token that differs from a previously cached prefix. Cache entries have a default 5-minute lifetime that is refreshed each time the cached content is used.

stem: A data team must classify 2 million archived documents overnight. The workload is not latency-sensitive and results can be collected the next morning. Which approach best optimizes cost while meeting the requirement?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes large asynchronous jobs at roughly half the standard token cost and returns results within 24 hours.
Send all requests synchronously in a tight loop and rely on prompt caching to bring the price down by 50%.
Use the streaming Messages API with a very high concurrency to finish faster and qualify for volume discounts.
Split the job across the Batches API and extended thinking so each document gets a larger reasoning budget for free.
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for high-volume, non-latency-sensitive workloads: it processes jobs asynchronously (target completion within 24 hours) at a 50% discount on input and output tokens, exactly matching this overnight, cost-sensitive use case. The prompt-caching option misuses caching, which discounts reused prefixes, not distinct documents. The streaming option is wrong because streaming does not grant volume discounts. The extended-thinking option conflates Batches with extended thinking; extended thinking increases token usage and cost rather than being free.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously, with each batch completing within 24 hours, and offers a 50% discount compared to standard API pricing.

stem: An architect is choosing a Claude model for a real-time chat routing step that does simple intent classification at very high volume, and separately for a low-volume legal-analysis feature requiring deep multi-step reasoning. What is the most appropriate right-sizing decision?
options: Use a fast, lower-cost model (Haiku tier) for the high-volume classification and a frontier model (Opus tier) for the complex legal reasoning.
Use the Opus tier for both to guarantee maximum accuracy everywhere and simplify operations.
Use the Haiku tier for both, adding extended thinking to the legal feature so the small model can match frontier reasoning.
Use the Sonnet tier for classification and the Haiku tier for legal analysis to balance cost across the two.
rationale: Model right-sizing matches capability to task: a fast, economical Haiku-tier model handles simple, high-throughput classification cheaply, while a frontier Opus-tier model is justified for low-volume, high-stakes reasoning. Using Opus for both wastes cost and adds latency by over-provisioning simple tasks. Using Haiku for both misassumes extended thinking makes a small model equivalent to a frontier model on genuinely hard reasoning. The Sonnet/Haiku split inverts the mapping, giving the harder task the weakest model.
referenceText: Anthropic offers a family of models balancing intelligence, speed, and cost; select the smallest, fastest model that meets your quality bar and reserve the most capable models for the hardest tasks.

stem: A team is designing the chunking strategy for a RAG pipeline over long technical manuals. Which practices improve retrieval quality? (Select 2.)
options: Preserve semantic boundaries (e.g., split on sections/paragraphs) and add modest overlap between adjacent chunks so context is not severed mid-idea.
Attach metadata such as source, section heading, and document title to each chunk so retrieved passages can be filtered and cited.
Use the largest possible fixed-character chunks with no overlap to minimize the total number of embeddings stored.
Strip all headings and structure before embedding so chunks contain only raw prose.
Embed each full document as a single vector to guarantee complete context is always retrieved.
rationale: Semantically aware chunking with light overlap keeps related ideas intact and avoids cutting concepts across boundaries, and per-chunk metadata enables filtering, ranking, and grounded citations. Huge fixed chunks with no overlap dilute relevance and sever context. Stripping headings discards structural signal that aids both retrieval and citation. One vector per whole document destroys retrieval granularity, returning far more irrelevant text than needed.
referenceText: Effective retrieval chunks content along semantic boundaries with some overlap and enriches chunks with metadata, improving both relevance and the ability to produce grounded, citable answers.

stem: During a design review, a stakeholder asks which Anthropic-native model call generates the vector embeddings for the RAG system. What is the accurate response?
options: Anthropic does not offer a first-party embedding model; the documentation recommends using a third-party embeddings provider (such as Voyage AI) to create vectors, then passing retrieved text to Claude.
The Messages API returns an embeddings array alongside every completion, so no separate service is required.
Claude's tokenizer endpoint doubles as the embedding model when called with an embed=true flag.
Extended thinking mode outputs the document embeddings as part of its thinking blocks.
rationale: Anthropic does not provide its own embedding model; its guidance points customers to embedding providers such as Voyage AI to generate vectors for retrieval, after which the retrieved passages are supplied to Claude for generation. The other options describe capabilities that do not exist: the Messages API returns text/tool output (not embeddings), there is no embed flag on token counting, and extended thinking emits reasoning text, not vectors.
referenceText: Anthropic does not offer its own embedding model. Anthropic recommends embedding providers such as Voyage AI for generating text embeddings used in retrieval workflows.

stem: A compliance-sensitive knowledge assistant must show exactly which source sentences support each claim in Claude's answer, with verifiable references rather than free-text 'Source: ...' strings that the model might fabricate. Which capability best meets this requirement?
options: Provide source documents and enable the Citations feature, which returns cited passages tied to specific locations in the supplied documents.
Instruct Claude in the system prompt to append footnote numbers, trusting the model to keep them accurate.
Lower the temperature to 0 so that any citations the model writes become guaranteed factual.
Enable prompt caching on the documents so the model memorizes and reliably reproduces their source identifiers.
rationale: The Citations feature lets you pass documents and have Claude return structured citations that reference exact locations in those source documents, giving verifiable grounding rather than model-invented references. Prompting alone can hallucinate footnotes. Temperature 0 confuses determinism with factual accuracy and does not prevent fabricated citations. Caching reduces cost/latency and has nothing to do with citation correctness.
referenceText: The Citations feature enables Claude to provide detailed references to the exact sentences and passages it uses from source documents, improving verifiability and reducing hallucinated sourcing.

stem: An architect is building an evaluation harness before shipping a prompt change to production. Which principles reflect strong empirical evaluation practice? (Select 3.)
options: Define task-specific success criteria and grade outputs against them (using code-based checks or an LLM-as-judge) rather than eyeballing a few samples.
Build a representative test set that includes edge cases and hard examples, not only easy happy-path inputs.
Run the eval automatically in CI so prompt or model changes are measured against the same benchmark over time.
Rely on a single manual spot-check of the newest example, since if the latest output looks good the change is safe to ship.
Tune the prompt directly on the held-out test set until the score is maximized, then report that score as the expected production accuracy.
rationale: Robust evaluation defines measurable success criteria, grades many cases automatically (code or LLM-judge), uses a representative set that stresses edge cases, and is run repeatedly (e.g., in CI) to catch regressions. A single spot-check gives no statistical confidence and misses regressions. Tuning on the held-out test set describes overfitting, which inflates the reported metric and destroys its value as an unbiased estimate of production performance.
referenceText: Create strong empirical evaluations: define clear success criteria, develop test cases that cover edge cases, grade outputs consistently with code or model-based grading, and run evaluations systematically to detect regressions.

stem: A team enables extended thinking to improve accuracy on multi-step math and planning tasks. Which configuration statement is correct?
options: The thinking budget_tokens must be less than max_tokens, and a larger budget generally allows more internal reasoning at higher latency and token cost.
Extended thinking is free of token charges because thinking blocks are internal and never billed.
budget_tokens can exceed max_tokens because thinking happens outside the output budget entirely.
Extended thinking must be enabled for every request to Claude or tool use will not function.
rationale: With extended thinking, you set a budget_tokens value that must be smaller than max_tokens; increasing it permits deeper reasoning but raises latency and cost because thinking tokens are billed as output. Thinking tokens are billed, so the 'free' option is wrong. The budget must be within max_tokens, so the 'can exceed' option is wrong. Extended thinking is optional and unrelated to whether tool use functions, so the 'must be enabled' option is false.
referenceText: When using extended thinking, you set a token budget for reasoning; the thinking budget must be less than max_tokens, and thinking tokens count toward billing and increase latency.

stem: An architect wants Claude to reliably distinguish instructions, a lengthy reference document, and the end-user's question within a single prompt so the model does not confuse them. Which prompt-engineering technique does Anthropic specifically recommend for this?
options: Wrap each distinct part in descriptive XML tags (e.g., <instructions>, <document>, <question>) to give the prompt clear, unambiguous structure.
Concatenate all sections into one paragraph and rely on the model to infer the boundaries.
Encode the entire prompt as base64 so structural tokens cannot bleed together.
Put every section in ALL CAPS so the model treats each with equal priority.
rationale: Anthropic recommends XML tags to delineate parts of a prompt (instructions, context, examples, input), which reduces ambiguity and lets you reference sections precisely. Concatenating into one paragraph removes the very structure the model needs. Base64 obscures meaning and hurts comprehension. ALL CAPS does not create structural boundaries and can degrade readability without organizing the content.
referenceText: Use XML tags to structure your prompts; tags like <instructions>, <example>, and <document> help Claude clearly separate different parts of the input and reduce misinterpretation.

stem: To force Claude to return only a JSON object with no preamble like 'Here is the JSON:', which technique is most direct and reliable?
options: Prefill the assistant turn with the opening '{' so the model continues the structured output from that point.
Add 'Please do not add any preamble' and hope the instruction is always followed.
Set temperature above 1.0 to suppress conversational filler.
Send the request through the Batches API, which strips non-JSON text automatically.
rationale: Prefilling the assistant response with the initial token(s) of the desired format (e.g., an opening brace) constrains Claude to continue in that structure, eliminating preamble deterministically. Relying on an instruction alone is less reliable than prefill. High temperature increases variability, not structure. The Batches API does not post-process or strip text from responses.
referenceText: Prefilling Claude's response by providing the beginning of the assistant turn steers output format and can eliminate unwanted preamble, forcing the model to continue from your provided prefix.
