Batch requests are billed at a 50% discount relative to standard Messages API pricing
Each request in a batch streams its response token-by-token to the caller as it generates
Batches are processed asynchronously and typically complete within 24 hours
rationale: Batch requests receive a 50% pricing discount and are processed asynchronously with a target completion window of up to 24 hours, so options 1 and 3 are correct. There is no sub-minute guarantee; batching explicitly trades latency for cost. Batched requests do not stream token-by-token; results are retrieved once the batch completes.
referenceText: Message Batches are processed asynchronously, complete within 24 hours, and cost 50% less than standard API requests.

stem: A product embeds three Claude-powered features: (a) high-volume real-time routing of inbound messages into 8 categories, (b) drafting nuanced legal-risk analyses of contracts, and (c) simple extraction of dates from receipts at scale. To right-size for cost and capability, which model assignment is most appropriate?
options: Use a fast, low-cost model (e.g., Haiku) for routing and receipt extraction, and a frontier model (e.g., Opus) for the legal-risk analysis
Use the largest frontier model for all three features to guarantee uniform quality
Use a fast, low-cost model for all three features to minimize spend
Use a frontier model for routing and extraction, and a low-cost model for the legal analysis
rationale: Right-sizing matches task complexity to model tier: high-volume, low-complexity tasks (routing, date extraction) run well on a fast, inexpensive model, while deep multi-step reasoning (legal-risk analysis) warrants a frontier model. Using the largest model everywhere wastes cost on trivial tasks; using the smallest everywhere under-serves the reasoning-heavy task. Option 4 inverts the correct mapping entirely.
referenceText: Choose the model tier that matches task complexity, latency, and cost requirements; use smaller models for simple high-volume tasks and larger models for complex reasoning.

stem: A team is building a RAG pipeline over a 400-page technical manual. Retrieval quality is poor because answers frequently span section boundaries and lose surrounding context. Which chunking strategy most directly addresses this?
options: Split on semantic boundaries (headings/paragraphs) and add overlap between adjacent chunks so boundary-spanning context is preserved
Split the manual into fixed 10-character chunks to maximize retrieval granularity
Store the entire 400-page manual as a single chunk so no context is ever lost
Remove all overlap and cut chunks strictly at fixed byte offsets regardless of content
rationale: Chunking on semantic boundaries with overlap keeps related content together and preserves context that straddles boundaries, improving retrieval relevance. Ten-character chunks destroy meaning and explode index size. A single giant chunk defeats retrieval and blows past useful context targeting. Fixed byte-offset cuts with no overlap are exactly the boundary-splitting behavior causing the reported failure.
referenceText: Effective retrieval chunks respect semantic boundaries and use overlap so that context spanning chunk edges is not lost.

stem: An architect wants to add semantic vector search to a RAG system that uses Claude for generation. Which approach aligns with Anthropic's guidance for producing the embeddings?
options: Use a dedicated embeddings provider (such as Voyage AI) to generate vectors, since Claude models are not an embeddings API
Call the Claude Messages API and read the embedding vector from the response metadata
Enable an embeddings=true flag on the Messages request to return vectors instead of text
Derive embeddings by averaging the token log-probabilities returned by Claude
rationale: Anthropic does not offer a first-party embeddings model and recommends a dedicated embeddings provider such as Voyage AI for vector generation. The Messages API returns generated text, not embedding vectors in metadata; there is no embeddings flag on the request. Averaging log-probabilities is not a supported or meaningful way to obtain sentence embeddings.
referenceText: Anthropic recommends using a specialized embeddings model provider such as Voyage AI; Claude itself does not generate embeddings.

stem: A regulated-industry customer requires that every claim in Claude's answers be traceable to specific source passages to reduce unsupported statements and enable auditing. Which native platform capability should the architect use?
options: The Citations feature, passing source material as document content blocks so responses reference the exact supporting text
A higher temperature setting so the model quotes documents more often
The stop_sequences parameter to force the model to end after each cited sentence
Extended thinking mode, which automatically inserts source URLs into every answer
rationale: The Citations feature lets Claude ground responses in provided documents and return references to the exact supporting passages, which is precisely what an auditable, traceable answer requires. Higher temperature increases randomness and would reduce, not improve, faithfulness. stop_sequences only halt generation and produce no citations. Extended thinking exposes reasoning but does not attach verified source references.
referenceText: Citations enables Claude to ground its answers in source documents and return references to the specific text that supports each claim.

stem: Before scaling a Claude feature to production, a team wants to know whether prompt changes actually improve output quality. Which practice best supports this?
options: Build a representative evaluation set with graded test cases and measure each prompt version against it empirically
Ship each prompt change directly to production and rely on user complaints as the signal
Judge quality by manually eyeballing three outputs from the newest prompt only
Assume any change to a longer, more detailed prompt is an improvement by default
rationale: A held-out evaluation set with graded cases gives an empirical, repeatable measure of whether a change helps or regresses, which is the recommended way to validate prompt changes. Relying on production complaints is slow and harms users. Eyeballing three outputs is not statistically meaningful. Assuming longer prompts are better ignores measurement entirely and often backfires.
referenceText: Develop empirical, test-driven evaluations with representative graded cases to measure whether prompt or model changes improve quality.

stem: An SRE team needs production observability so that when a user reports a bad Claude response, engineers can locate and investigate that exact API call with Anthropic support if needed. Which telemetry is most important to capture per request?
options: The response request-id header along with token usage, latency, model, and stop_reason for each call
Only the aggregate daily count of successful requests
A screenshot of the end-user's browser at the time of the call
The raw CA certificate used for the TLS handshake on each request
rationale: Logging the per-request request-id (plus usage, latency, model, and stop_reason) lets engineers correlate a specific user complaint to a specific API call and reference it in support escalations, which is core to LLM observability. A daily success count gives no per-call traceability. Browser screenshots and TLS certificates are unrelated to diagnosing a specific model response.
referenceText: Each API response includes a request-id; log it together with usage and latency to trace and debug individual requests.

stem: To comply with GDPR data-minimization when integrating Claude into an EU-facing app, which design choice is most aligned with the principle?
options: Send only the fields strictly necessary for the task and strip or pseudonymize personal identifiers before they reach the API
Forward each user's full profile and history on every request so the model always has maximum context
Persist every raw prompt and completion indefinitely in case they are useful later
Embed users' government ID numbers in the system prompt to personalize responses
rationale: Data minimization requires processing only the personal data necessary for the purpose, so sending just the required fields and pseudonymizing identifiers is the aligned choice. Forwarding full profiles, retaining all data indefinitely, and embedding sensitive IDs all expand personal-data exposure beyond what the task needs, directly violating the principle.
referenceText: Follow data-minimization practices: send only data necessary for the task and avoid transmitting or retaining unnecessary personal information.

stem: A healthcare provider wants to process protected health information (PHI) through Claude. What is the appropriate first step to support HIPAA compliance for this integration?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with Anthropic and configure the account for the agreed data-handling terms before sending PHI
Rely on standard consumer terms, since API traffic is encrypted in transit by default
Add a disclaimer in the app footer stating the service is HIPAA compliant
Hash the patient names in the prompt, which by itself makes the workload HIPAA compliant
rationale: HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement with any vendor that processes PHI on the covered entity's behalf, so executing a BAA and configuring the agreed terms is the correct prerequisite. Transport encryption alone does not satisfy HIPAA's contractual requirements. A footer disclaimer has no legal effect. Hashing one field does not de-identify a record or replace the BAA obligation.
referenceText: Customers handling regulated data such as PHI must have appropriate agreements (e.g., a BAA) and data-handling configuration in place before processing that data.

stem: Which statements about Anthropic's enterprise compliance and data-handling posture are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: Anthropic trains its foundation models on all commercial API inputs and outputs by default
Anthropic maintains SOC 2 Type II compliance for its services
Zero-data-retention arrangements are available for qualifying enterprise use cases
Every standard API account is automatically authorized at FedRAMP High
rationale: Anthropic holds SOC 2 Type II compliance and offers zero-data-retention arrangements for eligible enterprise customers, so options 2 and 3 are correct. Anthropic does not train foundation models on commercial API inputs/outputs by default. FedRAMP authorization applies to specific government-authorized offerings and is not automatically granted to every standard account.
referenceText: Anthropic does not train its models on commercial API data by default, maintains SOC 2 Type II, and offers zero-data-retention options for qualifying customers.

stem: A company already runs its infrastructure in AWS and wants to call Claude without managing a separate Anthropic API key, keeping traffic and access control inside AWS. Which integration path fits best?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, using AWS IAM for authentication and Bedrock model IDs for invocation
Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock but continue authenticating with an Anthropic API key passed in the header
Call the Anthropic first-party API and tunnel the responses into AWS via a VPC endpoint to gain IAM control
Use Google Vertex AI, since it federates automatically with AWS IAM roles
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock uses native AWS IAM for authentication and Bedrock model identifiers for invocation, keeping auth and governance within AWS. Bedrock does not use an Anthropic API key. Tunneling the first-party API does not confer IAM-based model authorization. Vertex AI is Google Cloud's platform and does not federate with AWS IAM.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock is invoked using AWS credentials/IAM and Bedrock model IDs rather than an Anthropic API key.

stem: A team standardized on Google Cloud and wants to invoke Claude with their existing GCP identity and regional controls. Which statement about using Claude on Vertex AI is correct?
options: Requests authenticate with Google Cloud credentials and target a specific GCP project and region where the model is available
Vertex AI requires you to supply an Anthropic-issued API key in place of Google Cloud credentials
Vertex AI ignores region selection and always routes Claude requests to a single global endpoint
Vertex AI can only be used after first proxying every request through Amazon Bedrock
rationale: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication and is invoked against a specific GCP project and region where the model is offered. It does not use an Anthropic API key. Region selection matters because model availability is region-scoped, and Vertex AI is independent of Amazon Bedrock.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI uses Google Cloud authentication and is called within a chosen GCP project and supported region.

stem: A production integration occasionally receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses under load. Which resilience pattern is the most appropriate response?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honor any retry-after guidance, and fall back to an alternate model or degraded path if failures persist
Immediately retry in a tight loop with no delay until the request eventually succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as fatal and surface a hard error to the user on the first occurrence
Increase the per-request max_tokens so the request is prioritized ahead of others
rationale: Transient 429/529 responses should be handled with exponential backoff plus jitter, respecting retry-after, and with a fallback path for sustained failures, which is the standard resilient-client design. Tight no-delay retries worsen congestion and can extend throttling. Failing hard on the first transient error needlessly degrades reliability. Raising max_tokens does not grant priority and increases load.
referenceText: Handle 429 and 529 responses by retrying with exponential backoff (and jitter), respecting retry timing, rather than retrying immediately or failing hard.

stem: An architect wants Claude-based agents to access internal tools and data sources (a ticketing system, a document store) through a standardized, reusable interface instead of bespoke per-integration code. Which technology is purpose-built for this?
options: The Model Context Protocol (MCP), where clients connect to servers that expose tools and resources over a standard protocol
A single monolithic system prompt that pastes all tool documentation inline for every request
Hard-coded HTTP calls unique to each backend, duplicated in every agent application
The Message Batches API, which standardizes access to external systems
rationale: MCP defines a standard client-server protocol for exposing tools and resources, enabling reusable, interoperable integrations across applications. A giant inline system prompt does not connect to systems or standardize access. Bespoke per-backend HTTP code is exactly the duplication MCP eliminates. The Batches API is for asynchronous bulk inference, not standardized tool integration.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard in which clients connect to servers that expose tools, resources, and prompts through a common interface.

stem: Anthropic announces that a Claude model version powering a live product will be deprecated on a future date. As the architect responsible for lifecycle and stakeholder management, what is the best course of action?
options: Track the deprecation timeline, test and validate a successor model against the existing evaluation suite, and communicate the migration plan and timeline to stakeholders before the retirement date
Wait until the model returns errors in production, then swap models reactively during the incident
Pin to the deprecated model indefinitely and assume it will remain available
Migrate silently to a new model with no regression testing or stakeholder notice to move quickly
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means monitoring published deprecation dates, validating a successor against your eval suite, and proactively communicating the migration plan to stakeholders ahead of retirement. Waiting for production errors guarantees an outage. Pinning indefinitely ignores the announced end-of-life. Migrating with no regression testing or communication risks silent quality regressions and erodes stakeholder trust.
referenceText: Anthropic publishes model deprecation timelines; plan migrations, validate replacements, and notify stakeholders before retirement dates.

stem: A production chatbot sends the same 9,000-token system prompt (product policies and few-shot examples) on every turn, followed by a short, varying user message. Using Claude Sonnet, the architect wants to cut input cost and time-to-first-token. What is the most effective way to apply prompt caching?
options: Add a cache_control breakpoint on the final user message so the entire request body is cached each turn
Rely on Claude to automatically cache any prompt longer than 1,024 tokens with no cache_control markers
Add a single cache_control breakpoint at the end of the static system-prompt content, keeping the varying user message after it
Add cache_control breakpoints on every message, including each user turn, to maximize the number of cache hits
rationale: Prompt caching caches the contiguous prefix up to a cache_control breakpoint, so the large static content must come first and the breakpoint must sit at its end, before the varying user text; this maximizes prefix reuse. Caching the varying user message yields no reuse because its content changes every turn. There is no automatic caching—cache_control must be set explicitly, and the minimum cacheable length for Sonnet/Opus is 1,024 tokens only when a breakpoint is present. Marking every user turn wastes cache writes (which cost more) on content that will not be reused.
referenceText: Prompt caching: cache breakpoints cache the prefix up to the marker; place static content before variable content.

stem: An enterprise must classify 500,000 archived support tickets overnight. The job is not latency-sensitive, and the team wants the lowest per-token cost while keeping code simple. Which approach best fits?
options: Send all requests to the standard synchronous endpoint using the priority service tier for guaranteed throughput
Submit the work through the Message Batches API, which processes asynchronously within 24 hours at roughly 50% lower cost
Stream each request individually to reduce time-to-first-token across the whole run
Rely solely on prompt caching to make the 500,000 synchronous requests cost-effective
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large, latency-tolerant workloads: it processes asynchronously (target within 24 hours) and provides about a 50% discount on input and output tokens versus standard requests. The priority tier optimizes for low latency at higher, not lower, cost. Streaming reduces perceived latency for interactive use but does not reduce cost and does not help an offline bulk job. Prompt caching lowers cost only for shared prefixes and does not, by itself, address bulk asynchronous throughput or pricing.
referenceText: Message Batches API: asynchronous processing within 24 hours at 50% cost savings for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: A team runs a high-volume intent-classification step where inputs are short and outputs are a single label. Their current pipeline uses Claude Opus and is too expensive and slow. Following model right-sizing guidance, what is the best first optimization to evaluate?
options: Keep Opus but lower max_tokens to 1 so the model is forced to answer faster and cheaper
Keep Opus and raise temperature to speed up sampling of the single label
Switch every step of the pipeline to Haiku without measuring, since Haiku is always sufficient for classification
Move the classification step to a smaller, faster model such as Claude Haiku and validate accuracy against a held-out eval set
rationale: Right-sizing matches task complexity to model capability: a bounded classification task is a strong candidate for a smaller, faster, cheaper model like Haiku, but the change must be validated against a held-out evaluation set to confirm accuracy is preserved. Lowering max_tokens caps output length but does not address the cost of the expensive model on input, and cannot force a valid label. Temperature changes sampling randomness, not speed or cost. Blindly switching the entire pipeline with no measurement is an unsupported absolute; some steps may need a larger model.
referenceText: Choosing a model: balance intelligence, speed, and cost; validate downsizing with evaluations.

stem: During a traffic spike, an application intermittently receives HTTP 529 responses from the Claude API. What is the appropriate resilient-design response?
options: Treat 529 as a transient overloaded condition and retry with exponential backoff and jitter
Treat 529 as an authentication failure and rotate the API key before retrying
Treat 529 as a malformed-request error and stop sending the request permanently
Immediately retry the same request in a tight loop with no delay until it succeeds
rationale: A 529 indicates the API is temporarily overloaded; it is transient, so the correct pattern is to retry with exponential backoff plus jitter to avoid synchronized retry storms. It is not an auth problem (that surfaces as 401), so key rotation is irrelevant. It is not a client validation error (400/422), so permanently dropping the request is wrong. Tight, delay-free retry loops worsen the overload and can amplify the outage.
referenceText: API errors: 529 overloaded is transient; retry with exponential backoff.

stem: An architect is designing the ingestion pipeline for a RAG system over long technical manuals. Retrieval quality is poor because chunks frequently split sentences and lose surrounding context. Which chunking adjustment most directly addresses this?
options: Increase chunk size to the full document so no context is ever lost
Reduce every chunk to a single sentence to maximize retrieval precision
Use semantically aware chunking with a modest overlap between adjacent chunks so context spans boundaries
Remove all overlap between chunks to eliminate duplicate tokens in the index
rationale: Semantic/structure-aware chunking that respects sentence and section boundaries, combined with a small overlap, preserves the context that spans chunk edges and improves retrieval relevance. Making each chunk the whole document defeats retrieval granularity and blows up token cost. Single-sentence chunks strip the surrounding context the model needs to answer. Removing overlap reintroduces the exact boundary-context loss the team is trying to fix.
referenceText: RAG/embeddings guidance: chunk on semantic boundaries with overlap to preserve context.

stem: A regulated customer requires that every answer produced by a document-Q&A assistant be traceable to the exact source text, and wants to reduce unsupported claims. Which Claude API capability most directly satisfies this?
options: Raise temperature so the model explores more of the source document
Enable the Citations feature so responses reference and quote the specific source passages used
Increase max_tokens so the model can restate the entire document in each answer
Disable streaming so the full answer is returned before any source is shown
rationale: The Citations feature grounds responses in provided documents and returns the specific cited source passages, giving verifiable traceability and reducing unsupported claims. Higher temperature increases variability, which works against faithfulness. Larger max_tokens lets the model output more text but does not create verifiable source links. Streaming settings affect delivery timing, not source attribution.
referenceText: Citations: ground responses in source documents and return the exact cited text.

stem: A team wants to measure whether a prompt change improved answer quality on an open-ended summarization task where exact-match scoring is impossible. Which evaluation approach is most appropriate?
options: Ship the change to all production traffic and watch for customer complaints as the primary signal
Have one engineer read a handful of outputs once and approve the change by intuition
Rely exclusively on token count as a proxy for summary quality
Build a graded eval set and use an LLM-as-judge with a clear rubric to score outputs at scale
rationale: For open-ended tasks where deterministic scoring fails, an LLM-as-judge with a well-defined rubric applied over a representative, graded eval set gives a scalable, repeatable quality signal you can compare across prompt versions. Shipping straight to all users makes the change itself the experiment and harms customers. A one-off ad hoc read is neither representative nor repeatable. Token count measures length, not quality.
referenceText: Develop tests: use eval sets and model-based grading for open-ended tasks.

stem: An application invoking Claude on Amazon Bedrock experiences throttling in a single region during peak hours, causing failures. Which Bedrock-native capability best improves resilience and throughput without the team managing failover logic themselves?
options: Use a cross-region inference profile so requests are automatically distributed across multiple regions
Hard-code a second region and manually switch by editing configuration during each incident
Request a higher rate limit and continue sending all traffic to the single region
Disable retries so failed requests surface immediately to end users
rationale: Bedrock cross-region inference profiles automatically route and distribute traffic across multiple regions, increasing available throughput and resilience without the customer building manual failover. Manually editing config per incident is slow, error-prone, and not resilient. A single-region quota bump does not add redundancy against a regional capacity event. Disabling retries removes a resilience mechanism and worsens user-visible failures.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock: cross-region inference distributes traffic across regions for throughput and resilience.

stem: A healthcare provider will send protected health information (PHI) to Claude and must remain HIPAA-compliant. Which combination of controls is required before processing PHI?
options: Simply add the word 'HIPAA' to the system prompt so the model treats data as protected
Rely on the model to detect and refuse any PHI automatically, requiring no contractual agreement
Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and enable zero-data-retention handling as configured for the covered account
Store PHI in the prompt but ask the model not to remember it between requests
rationale: HIPAA compliance is a contractual and configuration matter: a signed BAA must be in place, and the account must be configured for appropriate data handling (including retention controls) before PHI is processed. A system-prompt instruction has no legal or data-governance effect. The model cannot reliably self-detect all PHI, and detection is not a substitute for a BAA. Asking the model not to 'remember' data does not govern platform-level retention or provide the required legal agreement.
referenceText: Anthropic compliance: HIPAA support requires a BAA and appropriate data-handling configuration.

stem: Under GDPR data-minimization principles, a team is designing what to send in each prompt for a customer-support assistant. Which practice best aligns with data minimization?
options: Send the customer's full account record on every request so the model has maximum context
Send only the fields strictly necessary for the task and redact or tokenize identifiers that are not needed
Attach the entire customer database to the system prompt to avoid retrieval calls
Include raw personal identifiers in logs so support staff can debug faster
rationale: Data minimization requires collecting and processing only the personal data necessary for the specific purpose, so sending just the needed fields and redacting or tokenizing unnecessary identifiers is correct. Sending the full record, attaching the whole database, or logging raw identifiers all expand the personal data processed and retained beyond what the task requires, which conflicts with minimization and increases breach and compliance exposure.
referenceText: Governance and data minimization: process only the personal data necessary for the task.

stem: An SRE team wants reliable correlation between their internal logs and Anthropic support when investigating a failed API call. Which observability practice should they build into their client?
options: Log the full prompt and completion text of every request in plaintext to a shared channel
Disable all logging to reduce overhead and rely on Anthropic to retain full request bodies
Generate a random client-side UUID and assume Anthropic can look up requests by it
Capture and log the request-id returned in each API response so it can be provided to support for tracing
rationale: Each Claude API response includes a request-id; persisting it lets the team correlate their telemetry with Anthropic-side traces when escalating an issue. Logging full prompts and completions in plaintext to a broadly accessible location creates governance and PII risks and is not the correct tracing key. Disabling logging removes observability entirely. A client-generated UUID is not the identifier Anthropic indexes requests by, so it will not enable server-side lookup.
referenceText: API observability: responses include a request-id used to trace and debug requests.

stem: A team standardizing on Google Cloud wants to call Claude while keeping data governance and billing within their existing GCP project. Which integration is appropriate?
options: Access Claude through the Vertex AI integration, authenticating with Google Cloud credentials in their project
Proxy all Vertex traffic through a personal Anthropic API key stored in a public repository
Call the first-party Anthropic API and manually re-bill usage to GCP through spreadsheets
Use Vertex AI only for embeddings and route all Claude generation to a separate consumer account
rationale: Claude is available through Google Cloud's Vertex AI, where authentication and billing flow through the customer's GCP project, satisfying the governance and consolidation requirement. Embedding an Anthropic key in a public repo is a severe security failure. Using the first-party API and reconciling billing by hand defeats the reason to consolidate on GCP. Splitting generation onto a separate consumer account fragments governance and billing, the opposite of the goal.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI: access Claude via Google Cloud with GCP authentication and billing.

stem: An architect is deciding how to let a Claude-based agent access several internal systems (a ticketing tool, a search index, and a database) in a standardized, reusable way across multiple applications. Which approach best fits the Model Context Protocol (MCP) design intent?
options: Hard-code bespoke function-calling glue for each system inside every application separately
Embed database credentials directly in the system prompt so the model can query it
Expose each system as an MCP server so any MCP-compatible client can discover and call its tools through a common protocol
Fine-tune a model per system so no external tool interface is needed
rationale: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, resources, and prompts to models; wrapping each system as an MCP server lets any compatible client reuse those integrations through one protocol. Re-writing bespoke glue per application is exactly the duplication MCP eliminates. Putting credentials in the prompt is a security anti-pattern and does not provide a callable interface. Per-system fine-tuning does not give the model live, governed access to those systems.
referenceText: Model Context Protocol: an open standard for connecting models to tools and data via servers and clients.

stem: An architect is hardening a customer-facing Claude integration for reliability. Which practices improve resilience against transient upstream failures? (Select 3.)
options: Retry retryable errors (429, 529, 5xx) using exponential backoff with jitter
Set sensible client-side request timeouts and handle timeout as a distinct failure mode
Define a graceful fallback (for example, a smaller model or cached/default response) when the primary path is unavailable
Retry every error type, including 400 validation errors, indefinitely until success
Remove all timeouts so slow requests are never cut off
rationale: Resilient design combines backoff-with-jitter retries for genuinely transient errors, explicit timeouts with distinct handling, and a defined fallback path so the system degrades gracefully. Retrying non-retryable 400 validation errors indefinitely wastes resources and never succeeds because the request itself is malformed. Removing all timeouts lets a single stalled request hang the caller and exhaust resources, reducing rather than improving resilience.
referenceText: Rate limits and error handling: backoff on retryable errors; use timeouts and fallbacks.

stem: Anthropic has announced a deprecation date for the model version an application pins in production. What is the best lifecycle-management response for the architect?
options: Ignore the notice because pinned model versions are supported forever once deployed
Plan a migration to a supported model, run the existing eval suite against it to confirm quality, and cut over before the retirement date
Wait until the model returns errors in production, then swap models without re-evaluating
Immediately switch all traffic to the new model in production with no evaluation to save time
rationale: Deprecation notices signal a firm retirement timeline, so the architect should migrate to a supported model, validate it against the existing eval suite to catch behavioral regressions, and cut over before retirement. Pinned versions are not supported indefinitely. Waiting for hard failures causes an avoidable outage. Switching everything with no evaluation risks silent quality regressions in production; the eval gate is what makes the migration safe.
referenceText: Model deprecations: migrate to supported models ahead of retirement and validate with evaluations.

stem: Before rolling out a new feature, a finance team needs to estimate per-request input cost accurately for prompts of varying length, without incurring generation charges. Which tool is most appropriate?
options: Send each prompt to the model and read the usage from the completion, accepting the generation cost
Estimate cost by dividing the character count by four in application code and skip verifying it
Assume all prompts cost the same flat amount regardless of length
Use the token-counting endpoint to measure input tokens per request and multiply by the model's input price
rationale: The token-counting endpoint returns the exact input token count for a given request without generating a completion, so it enables precise cost estimation at no generation charge. Actually calling the model incurs the cost the team is trying to estimate ahead of time. A characters-divided-by-four heuristic is approximate and unverified, and can be materially off for code or non-English text. A flat per-prompt assumption ignores the strong dependence of cost on prompt length.
referenceText: Token counting: measure input tokens before sending a request for accurate cost estimation.

stem: A user-facing assistant produces long answers, and users complain about the wait before anything appears. The total generation time is acceptable, but the initial delay feels slow. Which optimization most directly improves the experience?
options: Enable streaming so tokens are displayed incrementally, reducing perceived time-to-first-token
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to produce a longer, more complete answer
Switch to the Message Batches API to lower the cost of each long answer
Raise temperature so the model begins responding more creatively and therefore faster
rationale: Streaming returns tokens as they are generated, so users see output almost immediately, which addresses the perceived-latency (time-to-first-token) complaint even when total generation time is unchanged. Increasing max_tokens can make responses longer and slower. The Batches API is asynchronous and worsens interactive latency. Temperature affects randomness, not latency or first-token timing.
referenceText: Streaming and reduce-latency guidance: stream responses to improve perceived latency.

stem: A team building a RAG system asks which embedding model to use, expecting a first-party Anthropic embeddings endpoint. What is the accurate architectural guidance?
options: Use the Claude messages endpoint to return raw embedding vectors by setting an embeddings flag
Every Claude model doubles as an embeddings model, so no separate provider is needed
Anthropic does not provide a first-party embeddings model; integrate a third-party embeddings provider (such as Voyage AI) for the retrieval step
Prompt caching replaces the need for embeddings in a RAG pipeline
rationale: Anthropic does not offer its own embeddings model and documents integrating a third-party provider such as Voyage AI for the embedding/retrieval stage; the correct design separates the embedding provider from the generation model. The messages endpoint generates text, not embedding vectors, and has no embeddings flag. Generative Claude models are not embedding models. Prompt caching optimizes repeated prefixes and does not perform semantic retrieval, so it cannot replace embeddings.
referenceText: Embeddings: Anthropic recommends third-party embedding providers (e.g., Voyage AI) for RAG.

stem: An architect is defining an evaluation program for a Claude feature before and after launch. Which practices reflect sound evaluation and testing methodology? (Select 2.)
options: Build a representative eval set with defined success criteria and version it alongside the prompt
Run evaluations continuously in a pipeline so prompt or model changes are regression-tested automatically
Judge quality solely from a single cherry-picked example that looks good in a demo
Evaluate only in production by watching aggregate traffic, with no offline test set
rationale: Good evaluation practice uses a representative, criteria-defined eval set that is versioned with the prompt, and wires evaluations into a pipeline so changes are automatically regression-tested. A single cherry-picked demo example is neither representative nor statistically meaningful. Relying only on production aggregates with no offline test set removes the ability to catch regressions before they reach users and makes root-causing changes difficult.
referenceText: Develop tests: representative, criteria-based eval sets integrated into a repeatable pipeline.

stem: A government contractor must meet FedRAMP requirements while using Claude and asks the architect for a compliant deployment path. What is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Add a note in the system prompt stating that all processing is FedRAMP-authorized
Assume any use of the public consumer API is automatically FedRAMP-compliant
Achieve compliance by encrypting the API key at rest on the client machine
Deploy Claude through a cloud platform that offers a FedRAMP-authorized environment (such as Claude via Amazon Bedrock in an authorized region)
rationale: FedRAMP is an authorization of a deployment environment, so a compliant path means using Claude through a cloud platform and region that carries the appropriate FedRAMP authorization, such as Bedrock in an authorized environment. A system-prompt statement has no bearing on authorization boundaries. The public consumer API is not, by default, a FedRAMP-authorized boundary. Encrypting an API key is good hygiene but does not confer FedRAMP authorization on the processing environment.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock: use an authorized cloud environment/region to meet government compliance requirements.

stem: A customer-support application sends the same 8,000-token product manual and system instructions on every request, followed by a short, unique user question. Latency and per-request cost are both concerns because traffic is high and bursty. Which optimization most directly reduces the cost and time spent re-processing the unchanging portion of each request?
options: Enable prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint at the end of the static manual and system instructions
Move the product manual into the Message Batches API so it is processed asynchronously
Switch the model from Claude Opus to Claude Haiku for every request
Reduce max_tokens on the response so the model generates fewer output tokens
rationale: Prompt caching lets you mark a large, stable prefix (system instructions plus the manual) with a cache_control breakpoint; subsequent requests that reuse that prefix read it from cache at a large discount and lower latency, which is exactly the repeated-static-context problem described. The Batches API optimizes throughput cost for non-latency-sensitive bulk jobs, not repeated identical prefixes on interactive traffic. Right-sizing to Haiku may hurt quality and does not address re-processing the same tokens. Lowering max_tokens only affects output tokens, not the repeated input.
referenceText: Prompt caching caches large stable prefixes for reuse across requests at reduced cost and latency.

stem: A nightly job must classify 500,000 archived documents. There is no user waiting on the results, and they only need to be available within 24 hours. The team wants the lowest possible cost. Which approach best fits these requirements?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API
Send synchronous requests in a tight loop and increase the account rate limit
Enable prompt caching and stream each response
Use the priority/higher service tier to guarantee fast processing
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant workloads and provides a substantial discount versus the standard synchronous API with a results window measured in hours, matching a 24-hour, no-user-waiting job. A synchronous loop is more expensive and fragile at that volume. Prompt caching helps with repeated prefixes but does not provide batch's bulk discount and streaming adds no benefit for an offline job. A higher service tier optimizes for latency, the opposite of what is needed, and costs more.
referenceText: Message Batches API processes large asynchronous workloads at reduced cost with a 24-hour completion window.

stem: During architecture review a stakeholder asks how to decide which Claude model to use for a new high-volume triage feature where most inputs are simple but a small fraction are complex. Which right-sizing strategy is most defensible?
options: Route the bulk of simple traffic to a smaller/faster model and escalate only the complex cases to a larger model, validating each tier against a shared eval set
Always use the largest available model so quality is never a concern
Always use the smallest available model to minimize cost, regardless of accuracy on complex cases
Pick the model with the largest context window, since context size determines answer quality
rationale: Model right-sizing means matching capability to task difficulty: serve the common simple cases cheaply/fast with a smaller model and escalate the minority of hard cases to a more capable model, with an eval set confirming each tier meets its quality bar. Always using the largest model wastes cost; always using the smallest sacrifices accuracy on the complex fraction. Context-window size is unrelated to per-task reasoning quality and does not by itself determine the right model.
referenceText: Choose the model that meets your quality bar at the lowest cost/latency, escalating harder cases as needed.

stem: A RAG system retrieves document chunks and passes them to Claude, but reviewers cannot tell which source sentence supports each claim in the answer. The team wants Claude to return verifiable references to the exact supporting text with minimal custom parsing. What should they use?
options: The Citations feature, providing documents as structured content blocks so Claude returns cited spans grounded in the source text
A lower temperature so the model stops hallucinating references
A larger embedding model to improve retrieval recall
Post-processing the answer with a regular expression to find quoted phrases
rationale: The Citations feature lets you pass source documents as content blocks and returns answers with citation objects pointing at the exact supporting spans, giving verifiable grounding without brittle custom parsing. Lowering temperature reduces variability but does not produce structured, source-anchored citations. A better embedding model improves what is retrieved but not the traceability of claims to text. Regex post-processing is exactly the brittle custom parsing Citations is meant to replace.
referenceText: Citations returns responses with references to the specific source spans that support each claim.

stem: A team is designing chunking for a RAG knowledge base of long technical articles. Retrieval quality is poor: relevant passages are often split so that a chunk contains half of an idea and loses the surrounding context. Which change most directly addresses this failure mode?
options: Introduce overlap between adjacent chunks and align chunk boundaries to semantic units such as sections or paragraphs
Increase the number of chunks returned per query to 100 regardless of relevance
Store the entire article as a single chunk so nothing is ever split
Remove all metadata from chunks to reduce token overhead
rationale: Overlapping chunks and boundaries aligned to natural semantic units (paragraphs/sections) keep each chunk self-contained so an idea is not severed across a boundary, which is the described defect. Returning 100 chunks floods the context with noise and raises cost without fixing boundary placement. A single giant chunk destroys retrieval granularity and precision. Removing metadata harms filtering/ranking and does nothing for the boundary problem.
referenceText: Effective chunking uses semantically coherent boundaries and overlap so retrieved chunks stay self-contained.

stem: Before launching a summarization feature, an architect wants to be able to tell stakeholders objectively whether a prompt change makes quality better or worse. Which practice best supports this?
options: Build a representative eval set with defined success criteria and grade candidate prompts against it, comparing scores across versions
Ship the change and rely on the absence of user complaints as evidence of improvement
Ask one engineer to read a few outputs and give a thumbs up or down
Only compare the token cost of the two prompts, since cheaper prompts are better
rationale: A representative, held-out eval set with explicit success criteria lets you score each prompt version and make an evidence-based, repeatable comparison, which is the foundation of empirical evaluation. Absence of complaints is a lagging, biased signal that misses silent quality regressions. One engineer skimming a handful of outputs is not representative or statistically meaningful. Cost alone ignores quality entirely and can reward worse answers.
referenceText: Define measurable success criteria and use empirical evaluations to compare prompt/model versions.

stem: An evaluation must grade open-ended answers where exact-string matching fails because there are many valid phrasings. The team wants a scalable, consistent scoring method beyond manual review. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Use an LLM-as-judge with a clear rubric and a fixed grading prompt, validated against a set of human-labeled examples
Require every candidate answer to match a single canonical string to be marked correct
Grade answers only by their length, treating longer answers as higher quality
Have the same model that produced the answer also assign its own final score with no rubric
rationale: For open-ended outputs where many phrasings are valid, an LLM-as-judge guided by a well-defined rubric scales grading consistently, and validating it against human labels calibrates its reliability. Exact-string matching fails precisely because of paraphrase variation. Length is not a quality signal. Letting the generating model self-score with no rubric introduces bias and gives no consistent standard; a fixed, rubric-driven judge (ideally a separate call) is the recommended pattern.
referenceText: For open-ended grading use rubric-based methods such as LLM-based grading, calibrated against human judgments.

stem: A production integration intermittently returns HTTP 429 responses during traffic spikes. The team wants a resilient client that maximizes successful completions without overwhelming the API. What is the recommended handling?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, and respect the retry-after header when present
Immediately retry the failed request in a tight loop until it succeeds
Treat 429 as a permanent failure and surface an error to the user without retrying
Raise max_tokens on the retried request so it is prioritized by the server
rationale: 429 indicates rate limiting; the resilient pattern is to back off exponentially with jitter and honor the retry-after header so the client spaces retries instead of amplifying the spike. A tight retry loop worsens congestion and can extend throttling. Treating a transient 429 as permanent needlessly drops recoverable requests. max_tokens does not affect scheduling priority and only changes generation length.
referenceText: On 429 rate-limit responses, back off exponentially and honor retry-after before retrying.

stem: An architect is documenting resilience for a real-time chat product. Occasionally the API returns a 529 (overloaded) error. Which client behavior is the most appropriate design response?
options: Retry the request after a backoff delay, and optionally fall back to a degraded response path if overload persists
Fail the whole user session permanently on the first 529
Disable TLS verification to bypass the overloaded endpoint
Send the identical request 50 times in parallel to increase the odds one succeeds
rationale: A 529 signals the service is temporarily overloaded; the correct design is to retry after a backoff and, if it persists, degrade gracefully via a fallback path so the user still gets a usable experience. Permanently failing the session over a transient condition is a poor resilience posture. Disabling TLS verification is a security violation and does nothing for overload. Blasting 50 parallel copies amplifies load and makes overload worse.
referenceText: 529 indicates temporary overload; retry with backoff and design graceful fallbacks.

stem: A chat UI feels slow because users stare at a blank screen until the full answer is generated. The total generation time cannot be reduced, but the team wants better perceived latency. What is the most effective change?
options: Stream the response so tokens render incrementally, reducing time-to-first-token perceived by the user
Increase max_tokens so the model has more room to answer
Switch every request to the Message Batches API
Add more retrieved context to each prompt so the answer is more complete
rationale: Streaming delivers tokens as they are produced, so the user sees output almost immediately and perceived latency drops dramatically even when total generation time is unchanged. Raising max_tokens can increase, not decrease, generation time. The Batches API is asynchronous and unsuitable for an interactive chat UI. Adding context increases input processing and latency while doing nothing for perceived responsiveness.
referenceText: Streaming improves perceived latency by returning output incrementally as it is generated.

stem: A healthcare provider will process protected health information (PHI) through Claude and must meet HIPAA obligations. Which measures are appropriate for a compliant, well-governed deployment? (Select 3.)
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider before sending PHI
Apply data minimization by sending only the PHI actually required for the task
Maintain audit logging of access and requests involving PHI
Publicly post raw PHI-containing prompts to a shared channel so the team can debug faster
Disable all encryption to reduce request latency
rationale: HIPAA-aligned deployments require a signed BAA covering PHI processing, data minimization so only necessary PHI is transmitted, and audit logging to demonstrate controlled access. Publicly posting raw PHI violates the minimum-necessary principle and confidentiality. Disabling encryption removes a required safeguard and is never an acceptable trade for latency.
referenceText: Handling PHI requires a BAA, data minimization, encryption, and audit logging.

stem: A European product must comply with GDPR. A user submits a valid right-to-erasure request. Which architectural practice best supports honoring such requests?
options: Design the system for data minimization and maintain a clear data map so stored personal data can be located and deleted
Store all raw prompts indefinitely in case they are needed for future model training
Rely solely on the model provider to erase the user's data from your own databases
Hash user identifiers once and assume hashing exempts the data entirely from GDPR
rationale: Meeting erasure requests depends on knowing where personal data lives; data minimization plus a maintained data map lets you locate and delete a subject's data across systems. Retaining all raw prompts indefinitely conflicts with storage-limitation and minimization principles and makes erasure harder. The provider cannot erase data held in your own stores. Hashing can still be personal data (pseudonymization, not anonymization) and does not automatically remove GDPR obligations.
referenceText: Data minimization and knowing where personal data is stored support GDPR rights such as erasure.

stem: A regulated enterprise standardized on AWS and wants to consume Claude while keeping traffic, IAM, and governance inside their AWS account boundary. Which deployment option best meets this?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock, using AWS IAM and Bedrock's regional endpoints
Call the public Anthropic API directly and forward the responses into AWS
Run the Claude model weights on the team's own EC2 instances
Use Google Vertex AI and peer it into the AWS VPC
rationale: Claude on Amazon Bedrock lets an AWS-standardized enterprise invoke Claude through Bedrock's API with AWS-native IAM, regions, and governance, keeping the integration inside the AWS boundary. Calling the public API directly moves auth and governance outside AWS. Anthropic does not distribute model weights to run on your own EC2. Vertex AI is the Google Cloud path, not an AWS-native option.
referenceText: Claude on Amazon Bedrock provides AWS-native access with IAM-based auth and regional endpoints.

stem: A team running Claude on Google Vertex AI reports that requests to a specific model fail while other models work. Which cause is most consistent with Vertex AI's model access model and should be checked first?
options: The specific Claude model has not been enabled/granted access in the Vertex AI Model Garden for that project and region
Vertex AI requires an Anthropic API key set in the Authorization header
Vertex AI only supports the Message Batches API, so single requests always fail
Prompt caching is mandatory on Vertex AI and the request omitted a cache breakpoint
rationale: On Vertex AI, individual Claude models must be enabled/granted in Model Garden per project and region, and requests are authenticated with Google Cloud credentials; a model not enabled in that region is the classic cause of selective failures. Vertex uses Google Cloud auth, not an Anthropic API key. Vertex supports synchronous requests, not batches only. Prompt caching is optional, not a request prerequisite.
referenceText: Claude on Vertex AI requires enabling the specific model in Model Garden per project/region and uses Google Cloud auth.

stem: To debug intermittent production issues, an SRE wants to correlate a specific failed Claude API call with Anthropic's logs when opening a support case. Which piece of information from each API response is most useful to capture in observability tooling?
options: The request-id returned with each API response
The response's stop_reason value
The number of output tokens in the usage object
The model's temperature setting for the call
rationale: Each API response includes a unique request-id (surfaced in response headers/SDK), which is the identifier Anthropic support uses to look up a specific call, so logging it is essential for correlation. stop_reason explains why generation ended but does not identify the request. Output-token counts and temperature describe the call's content and settings but cannot be used to locate a specific request in provider logs.
referenceText: Capture the request-id from responses to correlate a specific call with provider-side logs for support.

stem: An architect is defining an observability strategy for a Claude-based service to support cost control, quality monitoring, and incident response. Which signals are most valuable to capture per request? (Select 3.)
options: Input and output token usage for cost attribution
End-to-end latency including time-to-first-token
The request-id and error type for failed calls
The plaintext API key used, stored alongside each log line
A random guess of user satisfaction with no defined metric
rationale: Token usage enables cost attribution and right-sizing decisions, latency/TTFT tracks the user experience and SLOs, and request-id plus error type make failures diagnosable and correlatable with provider logs. Logging plaintext API keys is a serious security violation and must never be stored in logs. An undefined satisfaction guess is not a measurable signal and adds no observability value.
referenceText: Track token usage, latency, and request-id/error metadata; never log secrets like API keys.

stem: An MCP-based agent exposes tools to Claude. Before rolling it to production, the team wants confidence that Claude reliably selects the correct tool and passes valid arguments across realistic scenarios. Which testing practice is most appropriate?
options: Create an evaluation suite of representative user scenarios and assert on the tool calls and arguments the model produces
Manually invoke each tool once via the server and assume Claude will call them correctly in production
Verify only that the MCP server starts without errors
Rely on the tool descriptions being present, since well-described tools are always called correctly
rationale: Evaluating tool use means running representative scenarios and asserting that the model selects the right tool with valid arguments, catching selection and schema errors before release. Invoking tools directly tests the server, not Claude's decision-making. Confirming the server starts checks liveness, not correctness of tool selection. Good descriptions help but do not guarantee correct calls, so 'always' overstates the guarantee; empirical evaluation is still required.
referenceText: Evaluate agent tool-use by asserting on the model's tool selection and arguments across representative scenarios.

stem: A team wants to estimate and control input cost before sending large prompts, and to validate that a prompt fits within a model's context window. Which capability directly supports this without generating a full response?
options: The token counting endpoint, which returns the number of input tokens for a given request
Streaming, which reports token usage only after the full response completes
The Message Batches API, which counts tokens as a side effect of processing
Prompt caching, which prevents prompts from exceeding the context window
rationale: The token counting endpoint returns the input token count for a prospective request without running generation, letting you estimate cost and confirm the prompt fits the context window ahead of time. Streaming reports usage only after generation, which does not help pre-flight. The Batches API still generates responses and is not a lightweight counting tool. Prompt caching reduces cost of repeated prefixes but does not measure or cap context size.
referenceText: The token counting endpoint returns input token counts for a request before generation.

stem: An architect must communicate an upcoming model version migration to non-technical stakeholders and minimize regression risk. Which lifecycle approach is most sound?
options: Run the new model against the existing eval set, share a before/after quality and cost comparison, and pilot behind a flag before full rollout
Swap the model in production immediately and announce it after the fact
Delay any migration indefinitely because change always introduces risk
Let each engineer choose whichever model they prefer per request with no baseline comparison
rationale: Sound lifecycle management evaluates the new model on the established eval set, communicates an evidence-based before/after comparison stakeholders can understand, and pilots behind a flag to contain regressions before full rollout. Silent immediate swaps risk unmeasured regressions and erode stakeholder trust. Indefinite delay forgoes improvements and eventual deprecation forces the issue anyway. Ad hoc per-engineer model choices with no baseline make behavior inconsistent and unmeasurable.
referenceText: Validate model migrations against evals and pilot changes before full rollout; communicate results to stakeholders.

stem: A RAG pipeline sometimes answers confidently from chunks that are only loosely related to the question, producing subtly wrong answers. Retrieval returns the top-k by vector similarity only. Which improvement most directly targets this precision problem?
options: Add a re-ranking step over the initially retrieved candidates and instruct Claude to answer only from the provided context or say it does not know
Increase temperature so the model is more creative with the retrieved chunks
Remove the retrieval step and rely on the model's parametric knowledge
Embed the query with a different random seed on each call
rationale: Re-ranking the initial vector candidates raises precision by promoting the truly relevant passages, and instructing Claude to answer only from context (or abstain) curbs confident answers from loosely related chunks. Higher temperature increases variability and hallucination risk. Removing retrieval abandons grounding entirely and worsens factuality. Embeddings are deterministic for a given input; a 'random seed' has no meaningful effect and does not improve relevance.
referenceText: Improve RAG precision with re-ranking and by grounding the model to answer only from provided context.

stem: An enterprise agent sends a 12,000-token system prompt (compliance policies plus tool definitions) that is byte-for-byte identical on every request, followed by a short user turn that changes each call. The team wants to reduce both cost and time-to-first-token. What is the best way to apply prompt caching?
options: Place the static system prompt and tool definitions before the variable user content and set a cache_control breakpoint at the end of that static block, so later requests read the prefix from cache.
Put the variable user turn first and mark it with cache_control so it is cached and reused across requests.
Lower max_tokens on each request; caching engages automatically once the output is small enough.
Split the 12,000-token system prompt into roughly 60 separate 200-token cache breakpoints to maximize the number of cache hits.
rationale: Correct: prompt caching works on a stable prefix. Marking the end of the identical system/tool block with a cache_control breakpoint lets subsequent requests hit the cache (cache reads are billed at a small fraction of base input tokens) and lowers TTFT, while the changing user turn stays after the breakpoint. Option 1 is wrong because caching the variable content produces a different prefix each time, so it never hits. Option 2 is wrong: max_tokens governs output length and has nothing to do with caching, which is not automatic. Option 3 is wrong: there is a minimum cacheable length (roughly 1,024 tokens for Sonnet/Opus) and at most 4 cache breakpoints, so 200-token blocks fall below the minimum and exceed the breakpoint limit.
referenceText: Prompt caching lets you cache a stable prefix (system prompt, tools, documents) by placing a cache_control breakpoint on the last identical block; cache reads cost a fraction of base input tokens, there is a per-model minimum cacheable length, and up to 4 breakpoints are allowed.

stem: A team must classify 2 million archived support tickets overnight. The labeled results are consumed by analysts the next morning, no end user is waiting on any individual response, and reducing token cost is a priority. Which approach best fits these constraints?
options: Submit the workload to the Message Batches API, which processes requests asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at roughly a 50% discount on input and output tokens.
Fire 2 million synchronous Messages requests in a tight parallel loop to finish as quickly as possible.
Enable prompt caching with response streaming to minimize per-request latency.
Increase max_tokens and pack all 2 million tickets into a single request's context window.
rationale: Correct: the Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant workloads and offers roughly a 50% cost reduction with asynchronous processing that completes within 24 hours, which matches an overnight batch consumed the next morning. Option 1 optimizes for speed the team does not need, incurs full price, and risks rate-limit pressure. Option 2 optimizes latency (not the goal) and does not provide the batch discount. Option 3 is impossible: 2 million tickets vastly exceed any context window, and it also defeats per-ticket labeling.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a 50% cost discount, with batches completing within 24 hours; ideal for non-time-sensitive bulk workloads.

stem: Before launch, an evaluation on a held-out set shows Haiku scoring 0.94 and Opus scoring 0.95 on a high-volume intent-routing classification, with Haiku at a fraction of the cost and latency. What is the soundest recommendation?
options: Deploy Haiku for the routing classification and reserve larger models for the harder downstream tasks where evals justify the added cost.
Always deploy the most capable model available regardless of eval parity, to be safe.
Deploy Opus, because a 0.01 accuracy gap will compound catastrophically once traffic scales.
Skip the eval entirely and pick the model with the largest parameter count.
rationale: Correct: model right-sizing means letting task-specific evaluation drive model choice. When a smaller model reaches near-parity on a well-defined task, deploying it captures large cost and latency savings, and the capable model is reserved where evals show it is needed. Option 1 ignores empirical evidence and overpays. Option 2 asserts an unfounded compounding claim; the eval already reflects task performance, and a 0.01 gap does not justify the cost multiple without evidence. Option 3 discards the evidence-based process entirely and treats parameter count as a proxy for task fit.
referenceText: Choose the smallest model that meets your success criteria on representative evaluations; right-sizing balances capability against cost and latency per task.

stem: A legal RAG application must let reviewers verify every factual claim in the answer against the exact source passage, and fabricated references are unacceptable. Which capability best supports verifiable, grounded attribution?
options: Use the Citations feature by supplying source documents in the request so Claude returns claims linked to the specific document locations it actually used.
Append "cite your sources" to the prompt and rely on the model to generate bracketed reference numbers.
Set temperature to 0 so the model becomes incapable of hallucinating citations.
Post-process the output with a regex that attaches the lexically nearest chunk to each sentence.
rationale: Correct: the Citations feature has Claude reference the exact locations in the provided source documents that support each generated claim, giving reviewers verifiable, model-grounded attribution rather than free-text references. Option 1 relies on the model inventing reference markers, which can be fabricated. Option 2 is wrong: temperature 0 reduces randomness but does not guarantee grounded or accurate citations. Option 3 attaches sources by surface similarity after the fact, which does not reflect what the model actually used and can mislabel claims.
referenceText: The Citations feature grounds responses in supplied source documents and returns citations pointing to the specific passages used, improving verifiability.

stem: A production service intermittently receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses during traffic spikes. Which TWO practices together form a resilient retry and fallback strategy? (Select 2.)
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when the response includes one.
Treat every 429 and 529 as fatal and immediately fail the user's request with no retry.
Retry instantly in a tight loop until the call eventually succeeds.
Cap total retries and, once the budget is exhausted, fall back to a degraded response or a smaller-model path.
Disable TLS certificate verification to shave handshake time off each retry.
rationale: Correct: 429 and 529 are transient. Exponential backoff with jitter (respecting retry-after) spreads load and avoids synchronized retry storms, and bounding retries with a graceful fallback preserves availability when the backend stays saturated. Option 1 is wrong because these codes are retryable, not fatal. Option 2 (tight-loop instant retries) amplifies load and worsens overload. Option 4 is a security anti-pattern that does nothing for resilience and must never be done.
referenceText: 429 (rate_limit_error) and 529 (overloaded_error) indicate rate limiting and overload; the SDKs retry transient errors with exponential backoff honoring retry-after, and clients should bound retries with fallbacks.

stem: A healthcare product will send clinical notes to Claude to draft visit summaries. Which combination best aligns with HIPAA obligations and the principle of data minimization?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement with the provider, use a HIPAA-enabled organization, and strip identifiers not required for the task before sending the note.
Send complete records including all PHI so the model has maximum context, relying on the model to disregard sensitive fields.
Retain every prompt and completion indefinitely in plaintext to guarantee audit completeness.
Rely solely on TLS in transit, since at-rest encryption and retention controls are unnecessary for LLM API calls.
rationale: Correct: HIPAA-ready use of the Claude API requires a signed BAA and a HIPAA-enabled organization, which applies safeguards such as encryption, access controls, and audit logging; data minimization means sending only the PHI the task actually needs and removing the rest. Option 1 violates minimization and trusts the model with unnecessary PHI. Option 2 (indefinite plaintext retention) maximizes breach exposure and contradicts minimization. Option 3 ignores at-rest protection and retention governance, which are essential controls beyond transport encryption.
referenceText: The Claude API supports HIPAA-ready integrations: with a signed BAA and a HIPAA-enabled organization you can process PHI under safeguards such as encryption, access controls, and audit logging, sending only data necessary for the task.

stem: A team needs to measure the quality of open-ended, free-text summaries at scale, where exact-match grading against a single reference is meaningless. Which evaluation approach is most defensible?
options: Build a held-out eval set graded by an LLM-as-judge against a clear rubric, and validate the judge's scores against a sample of human ratings.
Read a handful of outputs each release and rely on subjective impressions to decide if quality is acceptable.
Use exact string match against one reference summary as the only quality metric.
Have the same model that produced each summary rate its own output from 1 to 10 with no rubric and no human validation.
rationale: Correct: for open-ended generation, a curated held-out eval set scored by an LLM-as-judge with an explicit grading rubric scales to many examples, and calibrating the judge against human labels keeps it trustworthy. Option 1 (vibes) is unrepeatable and non-quantitative. Option 2 is wrong because valid summaries can differ entirely from one reference, so exact match fails. Option 3 lacks a rubric and human validation, making the self-scores unreliable and uncalibrated.
referenceText: For subjective or open-ended tasks, use held-out eval sets with model-graded rubrics (LLM-as-judge) and validate grading against human judgments.

stem: To debug intermittent production failures and uphold latency SLOs on Claude API traffic, which observability practice provides the most value?
options: Log the request-id, model, token usage, and end-to-end latency for every call so failures can be correlated with the provider and latency percentiles tracked.
Log only the final user-visible text to keep storage costs minimal.
Track only average latency, since percentiles mostly add noise.
Rely on a client-side stopwatch for timing and do not capture any request identifiers.
rationale: Correct: capturing the per-request request-id lets you correlate specific failures with the provider's records for support and root-cause analysis, while logging model, token usage, and latency enables cost attribution and percentile (p95/p99) SLO tracking. Option 1 discards the metadata needed to diagnose failures. Option 2 is wrong because SLOs are defined on tail latency; averages hide the p95/p99 spikes users feel. Option 3 omits request identifiers, making failures hard to trace with the provider.
referenceText: Every API response includes a unique request-id header (also the request_id field in error bodies) used to correlate and debug requests with support; capturing usage and latency enables cost and percentile monitoring.

stem: A healthcare startup is building a patient-intake summarizer on the Claude API that will process protected health information (PHI). Their compliance officer asks what MUST be in place before any PHI is sent to Claude. Which action is the required first step to use Claude with PHI in a HIPAA-regulated workflow?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the model provider before sending any PHI
Enable prompt caching so PHI is stored in an encrypted cache rather than in prompts
Set max_tokens to a low value so less PHI is retained in outputs
Rely on Claude's built-in safety training to automatically redact PHI from responses
rationale: HIPAA requires a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with any vendor that processes PHI on your behalf; Anthropic offers a BAA to eligible customers, and it must be in place before PHI is transmitted. Prompt caching is a latency/cost feature and does not create a HIPAA-compliant relationship. Lowering max_tokens limits output length, not PHI exposure. Claude's safety training does not guarantee PHI redaction and is not a substitute for the contractual and technical safeguards HIPAA mandates.
referenceText: Anthropic Trust Center / privacy documentation on HIPAA and Business Associate Agreements

stem: An enterprise architect must confirm to their data-protection team whether prompts and completions sent to the Anthropic commercial API are used to train Anthropic's models by default. Which statement accurately reflects Anthropic's standard commercial API data-handling policy?
options: Inputs and outputs from commercial API traffic are not used to train Anthropic's models by default
All API traffic is used for training unless the customer files a manual opt-out ticket per project
Only prompts flagged by the safety classifier are excluded from training data
API data is always used for training but is fully anonymized before use
rationale: Under Anthropic's commercial terms, inputs and outputs from the commercial API are not used to train models by default; customers do not need to opt out. The other options misstate the default: training is not the default, exclusion is not conditional on classifier flags, and the policy is not 'always trained but anonymized.' Architects must communicate the correct default to satisfy data-governance and GDPR data-minimization reviews.
referenceText: Anthropic Commercial Terms of Service and Privacy Center on data usage for training

stem: A EU-based bank is subject to GDPR and wants to minimize personal data exposure in a Claude-powered RAG support assistant. Which design choice BEST embodies the GDPR principle of data minimization?
options: Redact or tokenize personal identifiers from documents before they are chunked, embedded, and sent as context
Increase the context window so the entire customer record is always available to the model
Store the full chat transcript indefinitely to support future model fine-tuning
Send all customer records to Claude and instruct the model in the system prompt to ignore any personal data
rationale: Data minimization means only the minimum necessary personal data should be collected and processed. Redacting or tokenizing identifiers before embedding and retrieval ensures PII is not unnecessarily transmitted or stored in vector indexes. Maximizing context, indefinite retention, and relying on a prompt instruction all increase—rather than minimize—personal-data exposure and processing, contradicting GDPR principles.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on embeddings and building RAG systems; GDPR data-minimization principle

stem: A U.S. federal agency requires that its generative-AI workload run in a FedRAMP-authorized environment. The team already uses AWS GovCloud. What is the most appropriate way to consume Claude while meeting this requirement?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock in an AWS region/environment covered by the required authorization boundary
Call the public Anthropic first-party API directly from GovCloud VPC endpoints
Self-host the Claude model weights inside the agency data center
Use the Message Batches API, since batch processing is inherently FedRAMP compliant
rationale: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock, which lets agencies inherit AWS's compliance authorizations (including GovCloud/FedRAMP boundaries) for the infrastructure layer. The first-party API is a separate environment and does not automatically fall within a customer's AWS authorization boundary. Claude weights are not distributed for self-hosting. The Batches API is a throughput/cost feature and confers no compliance authorization by itself.
