referenceText: Anthropic docs: Claude on Amazon Bedrock

stem: An architect is designing audit logging for a regulated Claude application so that every model interaction can be reviewed during an audit. Which logging approach best supports non-repudiation and traceability without introducing new compliance risk?
options: Log a request ID, timestamp, model version, actor identity, and a hash or redacted reference to inputs/outputs in an append-only store
Log full raw prompts and completions, including any PII, to a mutable application log for maximum detail
Log only errors, since successful requests do not need an audit trail
Rely on the provider's internal logs and keep no application-side records
rationale: Effective audit logging captures who did what, when, and against which model version, in a tamper-evident (append-only) store, while minimizing sensitive-data exposure via hashing/redaction—balancing traceability with data-minimization duties. Logging raw PII to a mutable log creates its own breach and integrity risk. Logging only errors loses the audit trail for successful (and potentially misused) requests. Relying solely on provider logs leaves the customer unable to independently demonstrate compliance.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on API request IDs, model versioning, and observability best practices

stem: A product team wants to launch a consumer-facing Claude feature that lets users generate marketing copy. Legal asks how to ensure the deployment stays within Anthropic's Usage Policy. What is the most appropriate governance control to implement?
options: Combine acceptable-use guardrails in the system prompt with automated moderation and a documented process for reviewing prohibited-use categories
Add a single line to the system prompt saying 'follow all laws' and consider compliance complete
Assume any output Claude produces is automatically policy-compliant because of safety training
Disable all content filtering to avoid over-blocking legitimate marketing requests
rationale: Compliance with the Usage Policy is a shared responsibility: architects should layer prompt-level guardrails, automated moderation/classification, and an operational review process mapped to prohibited-use categories. A single 'follow all laws' instruction is not an enforceable control. Safety training reduces but does not eliminate policy-violating output, so it cannot be assumed compliant. Disabling all filtering removes the very controls needed to prevent misuse.
referenceText: Anthropic Usage Policy (Acceptable Use Policy)

stem: A multinational company must keep processing of customer data within a specific geographic region for data-residency compliance. They deploy Claude via Google Cloud Vertex AI. Which configuration decision most directly satisfies the data-residency requirement?
options: Selecting the Vertex AI region that is within the required jurisdiction when issuing requests to Claude
Setting a system-prompt instruction telling Claude to keep data in-region
Choosing a smaller Claude model, since smaller models process less data
Enabling prompt caching, which keeps all data in the caller's local memory
rationale: On Vertex AI, the region (endpoint/location) you call determines where processing occurs, so selecting an in-jurisdiction region is the concrete control that satisfies data residency. A system-prompt instruction cannot change where computation physically happens. Model size does not affect processing location. Prompt caching stores cached prompt prefixes provider-side, not in the caller's local memory, and does not by itself guarantee residency.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Claude on Google Vertex AI (regional endpoints)

stem: In a RAG pipeline for a legal-research tool, stakeholders are worried that Claude may fabricate case citations, creating regulatory and malpractice risk. Which platform capability most directly reduces this risk by grounding claims in the provided source documents?
options: Use the Citations feature so responses reference the specific source passages that support each claim
Raise the temperature so the model explores more sources before answering
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to write longer answers
Switch to a larger embedding dimension to improve retrieval recall only
rationale: Claude's Citations feature returns references to the exact source passages used, making claims verifiable and reducing unsupported/fabricated assertions—a key hallucination-risk control for regulated use. Higher temperature increases variability and hallucination risk. More max_tokens affects length, not grounding. Larger embedding dimensions may help retrieval but do not, by themselves, tie generated statements to sources the way citations do.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Citations

stem: A high-availability Claude service occasionally receives HTTP 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) responses under peak load. Which resilience pattern is the recommended way to handle these transient conditions?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, respecting the retry-after signal, up to a bounded number of attempts
Immediately retry in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Treat every 429 and 529 as a fatal error and surface it to the end user
Permanently switch all traffic to a different model on the first 429
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; the recommended approach is bounded retries with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring rate-limit/retry signals to avoid thundering-herd amplification. Tight-loop retries worsen overload. Treating transient errors as fatal harms availability unnecessarily. Permanently rerouting all traffic after a single 429 is an overreaction that ignores the transient nature of the signal and can mask capacity planning issues.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: errors, rate limits, and retry handling

stem: An architect is designing a degraded-mode strategy so a customer-support assistant stays available even if the primary model is temporarily unavailable. Which fallback design is the most robust from a risk-management standpoint?
options: Define a documented fallback to an alternative Claude model (or provider endpoint) with tested prompts and clearly communicated capability differences
Cache the last successful response and always return it during any outage
Fail closed and return an error for all requests until the primary model recovers
Fall back to a model whose prompt format and safety behavior have never been tested for this use case
rationale: A robust fallback is pre-defined, tested against the same prompts/evals, and its capability and behavior differences are documented for stakeholders—so quality and safety are understood in degraded mode. Returning a stale cached response can produce incorrect answers. Failing closed for all traffic sacrifices availability unnecessarily. Falling back to an untested model introduces unknown quality and safety risk, defeating the purpose of a controlled fallback.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: models overview and handling availability with alternative endpoints

stem: Before deploying a Claude application into a regulated production environment, the risk committee requires evidence that the system was evaluated for safety and quality. Which practices should be part of a responsible pre-deployment evaluation? (Select 2.)
options: Build a representative evaluation set with graded test cases covering both typical and adversarial/edge inputs
Define measurable success criteria and acceptance thresholds agreed with stakeholders before launch
Rely solely on a handful of manual spot checks by the developer on launch day
Skip evaluation because Claude's built-in safety training makes application-level testing unnecessary
Measure only average latency, since correctness is guaranteed by the model
rationale: A defensible evaluation uses a representative, graded test set that includes adversarial/edge cases, and pre-agreed measurable success criteria/thresholds so pass/fail is objective and auditable. Ad-hoc spot checks are not reproducible or representative. Model-level safety training does not remove the need for application-level evals. Latency alone ignores correctness and safety, which are the primary concerns for a regulated launch.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: develop and run evaluations (test and evaluate)

stem: An agentic Claude application uses the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to expose tools that can modify production records and send external emails. From a governance and safety perspective, which control is most important for these high-impact tools?
options: Require human-in-the-loop approval and scoped, least-privilege permissions for destructive or externally visible tool actions
Grant the MCP server broad credentials so it never fails due to missing permissions
Allow the model to auto-approve all tool calls to minimize latency
Disable logging of tool invocations to reduce storage costs
rationale: High-impact tools (data mutation, outbound email) warrant human-in-the-loop confirmation and least-privilege, scoped credentials so an errant or manipulated model action cannot cause unbounded harm. Broad credentials violate least privilege and enlarge the blast radius. Auto-approving all tool calls removes the key safety checkpoint. Disabling tool-invocation logging destroys the audit trail needed for governance and incident response.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: tool use overview (permissions and human oversight for tools)

stem: An architect maintains a production application pinned to a specific Claude model version. Anthropic announces that this model is being deprecated with a retirement date. What is the most appropriate lifecycle-management response to communicate to stakeholders?
options: Plan a migration: re-run evaluations against the successor model, schedule cutover before the retirement date, and communicate expected behavior changes
Take no action, since deprecated models continue to serve traffic indefinitely
Immediately remove the feature from production the day the deprecation is announced
Silently switch to the newest model in production without re-running any evaluations
rationale: Model deprecation requires proactive lifecycle management: validate the successor against your eval suite, plan a cutover ahead of the retirement date, and communicate behavioral differences to stakeholders. Deprecated models are eventually retired, so inaction risks an outage. Pulling the feature immediately overreacts to an announcement with a future retirement date. Switching without re-evaluation risks undetected regressions in quality or safety.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: model deprecations and lifecycle

stem: During a vendor security review, a prospective customer's procurement team asks for evidence of Anthropic's security and compliance posture (e.g., SOC 2 reports, certifications). Where should the architect direct them to obtain this documentation?
options: Anthropic's Trust Center, which hosts compliance reports and certification details under appropriate access controls
The public model card, which contains the SOC 2 audit report as an appendix
The API rate-limits documentation page
The prompt-caching guide, since caching implies the relevant security controls
rationale: Compliance artifacts such as SOC 2 reports, certifications, and security documentation are made available through Anthropic's Trust Center, often under NDA/access controls. Model cards describe model capabilities and risks, not audit reports. Rate-limit and prompt-caching docs are technical references unrelated to compliance evidence. Directing procurement to the Trust Center is the correct, verifiable path.
referenceText: Anthropic Trust Center

stem: A Claude RAG assistant ingests documents from untrusted external sources (uploaded PDFs, web pages). The security team is concerned about indirect prompt injection embedded in retrieved content. Which mitigations meaningfully reduce this risk? (Select 2.)
options: Clearly delimit and label retrieved content as untrusted data, instructing the model to treat it as reference material rather than instructions
Constrain tool permissions so injected instructions cannot trigger high-impact actions without human approval
Increase temperature so the model is less likely to follow injected instructions
Trust retrieved content fully, since Claude's safety training blocks all injection attempts
Remove the system prompt so there are fewer instructions for an attacker to override
rationale: Indirect prompt injection is mitigated by (a) structurally separating and labeling untrusted retrieved content so the model treats it as data, not commands, and (b) least-privilege tool scoping plus human approval so any followed injection cannot cause high-impact actions. Temperature does not govern instruction-following susceptibility. No model reliably blocks all injection, so blind trust is unsafe. Removing the system prompt eliminates your own guardrails rather than the attacker's leverage.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on tool use security and mitigating prompt injection

stem: A platform team manages Claude API access for dozens of internal applications. From a security-governance standpoint, which API-key management practice best limits blast radius if one application is compromised?
options: Issue separate, scoped API keys per application/workspace and rotate them regularly with monitoring for anomalous usage
Share one organization-wide API key across all applications for simplicity
Embed the API key in client-side code so each app is self-contained
Never rotate keys, to avoid the operational overhead of re-deploying applications
rationale: Per-application scoped keys with regular rotation and usage monitoring contain the impact of any single compromise and enable precise revocation—core least-privilege practice. A shared org-wide key means one leak compromises everything. Embedding keys in client-side code exposes them to end users. Never rotating keys leaves a compromised credential valid indefinitely, maximizing exposure.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on API keys, workspaces, and access management

stem: An observability lead is instrumenting a production Claude service so the team can detect quality regressions, cost anomalies, and safety incidents. Which signals are the most valuable to capture for governance and operations? (Select 2.)
options: Per-request token usage (input/output, including cache read/write) and cost attribution by feature or tenant
Rates of stop_reason values, refusals, errors, and latency percentiles over time
Only the total number of API calls per month, with no per-request detail
The developer's subjective impression of quality recorded once at launch
The color theme of the dashboard used to view metrics
rationale: Token/cost attribution (including prompt-cache reads/writes) enables cost-anomaly detection and chargeback, while tracking stop_reason distributions, refusal/error rates, and latency percentiles surfaces quality and safety regressions over time. A single monthly call count lacks the granularity to attribute or diagnose issues. A one-time subjective launch impression is not an ongoing signal. Dashboard styling is irrelevant to governance signals.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on token counting, stop reasons, and prompt caching metrics

stem: A moderation team wants defense-in-depth for a public Claude chatbot that could receive harmful requests. Which architecture best reflects layered safety rather than relying on a single control?
options: Combine input screening, model-level guardrails via system prompt, and output moderation, with logging and escalation for flagged cases
Rely exclusively on the model to refuse harmful requests with no input or output checks
Only screen inputs, since anything that passes input screening is guaranteed safe to return
Only screen outputs, and pass all inputs directly to the model regardless of content
rationale: Defense-in-depth layers independent controls—input screening, model-level guardrails, and output moderation—plus logging and human escalation, so no single failure is catastrophic. Relying only on the model's refusals, only on input screening, or only on output moderation each leaves a gap that a determined or novel attack can exploit. Layering is the recommended safety architecture for public-facing deployments.
referenceText: Anthropic docs on content moderation and guardrails

stem: A team processes 500,000 non-urgent document classifications nightly and wants to reduce cost while keeping the workload governable and auditable. Which approach aligns cost optimization with sound governance for this batch workload?
options: Use the Message Batches API for the asynchronous jobs and retain per-batch request IDs and results for auditability
Send all 500,000 requests synchronously at once to finish as fast as possible
Use the largest available model for every document to be safe, regardless of task difficulty
Disable all logging during the batch run to save storage and speed up processing
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for high-volume, non-time-sensitive workloads at reduced cost, and retaining per-request IDs/results preserves auditability. Firing 500k synchronous requests risks rate limits and provides no cost benefit. Always using the largest model ignores model right-sizing and inflates cost. Disabling logging removes the audit trail needed for governance and error investigation, undermining accountability for a large automated job.
referenceText: Anthropic docs: Message Batches API (batch processing)

stem: A risk officer asks how the organization should handle suspected misuse or abuse discovered in a production Claude application (e.g., users attempting prohibited activity). Which practice best establishes accountable incident handling?
options: Maintain an incident-response process with monitoring, defined escalation paths, retained evidence, and reporting consistent with Anthropic's Usage Policy
Delete all related logs immediately to reduce the organization's liability
Take no action unless Anthropic contacts the organization first
Publicly disclose the affected users' identities to deter future abuse
rationale: Accountable incident handling requires proactive monitoring, defined escalation, evidence retention, and reporting aligned with the Usage Policy—so misuse is detected, contained, and documented. Deleting logs destroys evidence and can worsen legal exposure. Waiting for the provider to make first contact is not a control. Publicly disclosing user identities creates privacy and legal violations rather than a legitimate deterrent.
referenceText: Anthropic Usage Policy and enforcement/reporting expectations

stem: An intake application forwards free-text customer complaints to Claude for summarization. Legal review finds the complaints frequently contain national ID numbers that are not needed to produce a summary. Which design change best satisfies the data-minimization principle?
options: Redact or strip the identifiers that the summarization task does not require before the text is sent to the API
Send the full text unchanged and open a support ticket asking the provider to delete the identifiers after processing
Encrypt the request payload with a customer-managed key so the identifiers are protected while in transit
Raise max_tokens so Claude can echo the identifiers and then omit them from the final summary
rationale: Data minimization means collecting and transmitting only the personal data actually needed for the purpose. Removing identifiers not required for summarization before they ever leave your boundary is the correct control. Post-hoc deletion (option 1) still means the data was transmitted and processed, so minimization already failed. Encryption in transit (option 2) protects confidentiality but does not reduce the data collected. Echoing identifiers through the model (option 3) increases exposure rather than minimizing it.
referenceText: Data minimization requires limiting personal data to what is necessary; strip or redact unneeded identifiers before processing.

stem: A digital-health company intends to send protected health information (PHI) to Claude to draft clinical visit summaries. Which prerequisite must be in place to handle PHI in a HIPAA-aligned manner?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and enable the available data-handling controls, such as zero data retention
Rely on the default consumer terms because API traffic is already encrypted with TLS
Set a custom HTTP header of hipaa=true on every Messages API request
Proceed on the standard free tier after obtaining patient consent, since PHI cannot be anonymized
rationale: HIPAA requires a Business Associate Agreement with any vendor that processes PHI, combined with appropriate safeguards such as zero data retention where offered. TLS encryption (option 1) is necessary but not sufficient and does not create the required contractual relationship. There is no hipaa header (option 2) that establishes compliance. Patient consent (option 3) does not substitute for a BAA and appropriate technical/contractual controls.
referenceText: Handling PHI with a vendor requires a signed BAA plus appropriate safeguards; contact the provider's compliance/trust program.

stem: A US federal agency must run a Claude-based workload inside a FedRAMP-authorized boundary. Which approach best satisfies that requirement?
options: Access Claude through Amazon Bedrock in an environment carrying the required FedRAMP authorization, inheriting the platform's compliance boundary
Call the public Anthropic API directly and layer the agency's own encryption on top
Self-host the model weights in the agency data center to avoid any cloud dependency
Use the consumer Claude app and restrict access to agency-issued laptops
rationale: Consuming Claude via a cloud platform such as Amazon Bedrock lets the workload inherit that platform's FedRAMP authorization boundary, which is how federal agencies typically meet the requirement. The public API (option 1) is not the same authorized boundary. Anthropic does not distribute model weights for self-hosting (option 2). The consumer app (option 3) is not designed for FedRAMP-authorized agency workloads.
referenceText: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock, letting workloads run within the cloud platform's compliance boundary.

stem: A regulated deployment must keep an audit trail that links every Claude response back to who requested it and when. Which practice best supports this requirement?
options: Record the API response request_id alongside the authenticated user, timestamp, model, and token usage in your own append-only audit store
Store the full prompt and response only inside the provider's usage dashboard
Enable Claude's built-in per-user audit ledger by passing an audit=true request parameter
Depend on TLS access logs emitted by your load balancer
rationale: Each API response includes a request_id that you can persist together with the authenticated principal, timestamp, and model/usage metadata to build an auditable trail you control. The provider dashboard (option 1) is not an audit system you own and control retention over. There is no audit=true parameter (option 2). Load-balancer TLS logs (option 3) record connections, not which user produced which model output.
referenceText: API responses carry a request_id you can log with your own user, timestamp, and usage metadata for auditing.

stem: A product team asks who is responsible for making sure an application built on Claude complies with Anthropic's Usage Policy. Which statement is correct?
options: The deploying organization must enforce acceptable use in its own application, implementing safeguards appropriate to its use case
The provider automatically blocks every policy-violating use, so application-level controls are unnecessary
The Usage Policy applies only to consumer-facing products, not to internal tools
The Usage Policy governs only training data and does not apply to API applications
rationale: The deploying organization is accountable for how it uses the model and must build safeguards suited to its use case in line with the Usage Policy. Provider-side safety measures exist but do not remove the deployer's obligation (option 1). The policy applies regardless of whether a product is internal or consumer-facing (option 2), and it governs applications built on the API, not just training data (option 3).
referenceText: Applications built on Claude must comply with the Usage Policy; deployers are responsible for enforcing acceptable use.

stem: A support assistant sends the same 8,000-token policy-and-tone system prompt on every request, and the team wants to cut both cost and time-to-first-token without changing behavior. Which technique is most appropriate?
options: Apply prompt caching with a cache_control breakpoint on the stable prefix so repeated tokens are served from cache at a reduced rate
Move the policy text into every user message so it compresses better
Lower max_tokens on each request to shorten processing time
Increase temperature so the model spends fewer tokens deliberating
rationale: Prompt caching marks a stable prefix so its tokens are reused across requests, lowering cost and latency for the repeated content without changing the model's behavior. Relocating the text to user messages (option 1) does not reduce token processing. Lowering max_tokens (option 2) caps output length, not the cost of the large repeated prefix. Temperature (option 3) affects sampling, not token count or caching.
referenceText: Prompt caching reuses a stable prompt prefix across requests, reducing cost and latency for repeated content.

stem: A nightly pipeline must classify 500,000 documents. There is no real-time requirement and cost is the primary constraint. Which approach fits best?
options: Submit the work through the Message Batches API for asynchronous processing at a lower per-token cost
Fan out 500,000 concurrent synchronous Messages requests to finish as fast as possible
Send one enormous request containing all 500,000 documents to save on overhead
Run the job interactively and retry any request that is rate limited without backoff
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, non-time-sensitive workloads and offers reduced cost for asynchronous processing, making it the right fit. Massive synchronous fan-out (option 1) risks rate limits and higher cost. A single giant request (option 2) exceeds context limits and is not how batching works. Interactive retries without backoff (option 3) will worsen throttling.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large asynchronous workloads at reduced cost for non-time-sensitive jobs.

stem: A high-volume ticket-routing feature has a strict latency and cost budget. The team currently uses a large model. What is the most defensible right-sizing decision?
options: Evaluate a smaller, faster model against the required accuracy bar and adopt it if it meets the target
Always use the largest available model because it maximizes accuracy in every case
Pick the cheapest model without evaluation because routing is a simple task
Alternate randomly between models per request to average out cost and quality
rationale: Model right-sizing means selecting the smallest, cheapest model that still clears your measured quality bar for the task, verified with an evaluation. Defaulting to the largest model (option 1) wastes cost and latency. Choosing the cheapest without evaluation (option 2) risks unacceptable accuracy. Randomly alternating (option 3) produces inconsistent quality and is not a principled selection.
referenceText: Choose the model that meets your task's quality bar at the best cost/latency; validate the choice with evaluation.

stem: Under bursty traffic your service intermittently receives HTTP 429 responses from the API. Which client behavior is the correct resilient response?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring the retry-after header when present
Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request eventually succeeds
Treat 429 as fatal and surface an error to the user without retrying
Permanently lower your concurrency to one request at a time
rationale: A 429 indicates rate limiting; the resilient pattern is exponential backoff with jitter while respecting any retry-after guidance. Tight-loop retries (option 1) amplify the overload. Treating 429 as fatal (option 2) discards a transient, retryable condition. Dropping to single concurrency permanently (option 3) needlessly cripples throughput instead of adapting to bursts.
referenceText: Rate-limit (429) responses should be retried with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring retry-after.

stem: You are hardening a customer-facing Claude integration against provider-side incidents and latency spikes. Which two patterns improve resilience? (Select 2.)
options: Configure sensible request timeouts and retry transient failures with exponential backoff and jitter
Define a fallback path, such as an alternate model or a cached/deterministic response, when the primary call fails or times out
Remove all timeouts so that slow requests always eventually complete
Retry non-idempotent write operations immediately without any backoff
Route every request through a single dependency to make debugging simpler
rationale: Timeouts plus backoff-with-jitter retries prevent thread exhaustion and thundering herds during transient failures, and a defined fallback keeps the experience degraded-but-working when the primary path is down. Removing timeouts (option 2) lets slow calls pile up and exhaust resources. Immediate no-backoff retries of non-idempotent writes (option 3) can duplicate side effects and worsen load. A single dependency with no fallback (option 4) is a single point of failure, the opposite of resilience.
referenceText: Resilient designs use timeouts, backoff-with-jitter retries, and fallback paths for provider errors and latency spikes.

stem: A RAG-based policy assistant must let reviewers verify that each answer is grounded in source documents and reduce the risk of unsupported claims. Which capability most directly addresses this?
options: Use the Citations feature so Claude attributes statements to the specific source passages it relied on
Raise temperature so the model produces more varied and creative answers
Increase max_tokens so answers are longer and include more detail
Disable retrieval and rely on the model's parametric knowledge for consistency
rationale: Citations tie generated statements to the exact source passages, giving reviewers an auditable grounding and reducing unsupported claims. Higher temperature (option 1) increases variability and hallucination risk. More tokens (option 2) does not establish grounding. Removing retrieval (option 3) discards the authoritative sources the assistant is supposed to cite.
referenceText: The Citations feature grounds Claude's statements in provided source documents for verifiable, auditable answers.

stem: In a multi-tenant RAG system, a user retrieved a chunk that contained another tenant's confidential data. What is the correct root-cause fix?
options: Enforce tenant-scoped access control and metadata filtering at retrieval time so only that tenant's authorized chunks are returned
Reduce the number of retrieved chunks (top_k) so cross-tenant leakage is less likely
Instruct Claude in the system prompt to ignore any data belonging to other tenants
Re-embed all documents with a higher-dimensional embedding model
rationale: Cross-tenant leakage is an authorization failure in the retrieval layer; the fix is enforcing tenant isolation via access control and metadata filters so unauthorized chunks are never candidates. Lowering top_k (option 1) only changes the probability of leakage, not the boundary. A prompt instruction (option 2) cannot be relied on for access control after the data is already exposed to the model. Different embeddings (option 3) do not enforce isolation.
referenceText: Retrieval in multi-tenant systems must enforce access control and metadata filtering so only authorized chunks are returned.

stem: You are connecting Claude to internal systems through Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Which control most reduces the risk of over-privileged or unintended tool actions?
options: Apply least-privilege scoping to each MCP server and require human approval before high-impact actions execute
Grant each MCP server broad administrative credentials to avoid permission errors during rollout
Expose all available tools to the model so it can always choose the most capable one
Disable logging on MCP calls to reduce overhead in production
rationale: Least-privilege scoping limits the blast radius of any single tool, and human-in-the-loop approval for high-impact actions guards against unintended or injected commands. Broad admin credentials (option 1) maximize damage from any error or injection. Exposing every tool (option 2) enlarges the attack surface. Disabling logging (option 3) removes the observability needed to detect and investigate misuse.
referenceText: MCP integrations should follow least privilege and gate high-impact tool actions behind human approval.

stem: An agent fetches third-party web pages and passes their content to Claude, which can also call internal tools. A fetched page contains hidden text instructing the model to exfiltrate data. What is the most effective mitigation for this indirect prompt injection?
options: Deliver third-party content only inside tool_result blocks marked as untrusted, keep it separate from your instructions, and scope tool permissions to least privilege
Trust the fetched content because your own system retrieved it from a known URL
Concatenate the page text directly into the system prompt so the model weighs it as authoritative context
Raise the model's temperature so it is less likely to follow embedded instructions
rationale: The recommended defense is to treat retrieved/tool content as untrusted data, place it in tool_result blocks distinct from your trusted instructions, state an untrusted-content policy, and limit tool access so a successful injection can do little. Trusting content because you fetched it (option 1) ignores that the source is attacker-controlled. Putting it in the system prompt (option 2) elevates untrusted text to authoritative instructions. Temperature (option 3) does not address injection.
referenceText: Put untrusted third-party content in tool_result blocks, keep it separate from instructions, and apply least-privilege tool access.

stem: Before launching a customer-facing assistant, a team wants to systematically catch harmful or policy-violating outputs and prevent regressions as prompts change. Which practice best achieves this?
options: Build an evaluation suite with representative and adversarial test cases graded against defined criteria, and run it before and after every change
Manually spot-check a handful of outputs once, on launch day
Rely on the model's built-in safety training and skip application-level testing
Wait for user-reported incidents in production, then patch prompts reactively
rationale: A repeatable evaluation suite with adversarial cases and explicit grading criteria detects harmful outputs and guards against regressions whenever prompts or models change. One-time spot checks (option 1) do not cover adversarial inputs or future changes. Relying solely on built-in safety (option 2) ignores application-specific risks. Purely reactive patching (option 3) exposes users to harm before it is caught.
referenceText: Develop empirical tests with representative and adversarial cases and grade outputs against defined criteria before and after changes.

stem: A governance-minded team is defining what to monitor for a production Claude service to support SLOs and cost accountability. Which three signals are most relevant? (Select 3.)
options: Per-request token usage and the resulting cost
Latency, including time-to-first-token for streamed responses
Error and refusal rates over time
The exact wording of the provider's internal system prompts
The provider company's daily stock price
rationale: Token usage/cost, latency including TTFT, and error/refusal rates are the operational and cost signals that let a team enforce SLOs and control spend. The provider's internal system prompts (option 3) are neither available nor relevant to your SLOs. The provider's stock price (option 4) has no bearing on service health or cost governance.
referenceText: Monitor latency (including time-to-first-token), error/refusal rates, and token usage/cost for production governance.

stem: A compliance requirement states that the model provider must not retain the organization's prompts or outputs after a response is returned. Which option correctly addresses this?
options: Arrange zero data retention terms with the provider so inputs and outputs are not stored after the response is delivered
Add store=false to the request body to disable retention per call
Delete your own application logs nightly, which also purges the provider's copies
Encrypt the payload in transit, which prevents the provider from storing it
rationale: Provider-side retention is governed by contractual data-handling terms; zero data retention arrangements ensure inputs/outputs are not stored after processing. There is no store=false request field that controls provider retention (option 1). Deleting your own logs (option 2) has no effect on provider-side storage. Transit encryption (option 3) protects data in motion but does not determine retention.
referenceText: Provider data retention is governed by contractual terms; zero data retention agreements prevent storage after processing.

stem: An EU-based customer requires that its data be processed within an EU region to meet data-residency obligations while using Claude. What is the appropriate architecture?
options: Deploy Claude through a cloud platform such as Google Vertex AI or Amazon Bedrock in an EU region that satisfies the residency requirement
Call the global public API and add a region=eu header to pin processing to Europe
Store the results in an EU database after processing, which satisfies residency for the whole flow
Rotate requests across all global endpoints so no single non-EU region dominates
rationale: Consuming Claude via a cloud platform lets you select an EU region so processing occurs where residency rules require. There is no region header on the public API that guarantees processing location (option 1). Storing outputs in the EU (option 2) does not control where the inference itself happened. Rotating across global endpoints (option 3) sends data outside the EU, violating residency.
referenceText: Claude is available through Google Vertex AI and Amazon Bedrock, letting you choose a region to meet data-residency needs.

stem: The provider announces that a model version your production system depends on will be retired on a set date. Which lifecycle-management practice is correct?
options: Track the deprecation notice, evaluate the successor model against your existing eval suite, and migrate before the retirement date
Pin to the deprecated model version indefinitely so behavior never changes
Switch immediately to the newest model without running your evaluations to save time
Wait until the model is fully retired and errors appear in production, then react
rationale: Sound lifecycle management means monitoring deprecation notices, validating the successor against your evaluation suite to catch behavior changes, and migrating ahead of the retirement date. Pinning indefinitely (option 1) is impossible once the model is retired. Switching without evaluation (option 2) risks silent quality regressions. Waiting for production errors (option 3) guarantees an outage.
referenceText: Track model deprecation timelines and migrate to successor models with evaluation before the retirement date.

stem: A community platform needs to block clearly disallowed user submissions before they reach a downstream workflow that takes action. Which design is most appropriate?
options: Add a lightweight input-classification/moderation step, such as a fast Claude-based content filter, ahead of the main workflow
Let the main workflow run and rely on a human to review every action after it is taken
Increase the main model's temperature so unsafe requests are answered less coherently
Trust the primary model to refuse everything harmful, so no separate screening is needed
rationale: A dedicated moderation/classification stage in front of the workflow screens inputs and blocks disallowed content before any action is taken, which is the recommended pattern. Post-hoc human review of every action (option 1) does not prevent the harmful action and does not scale. Temperature (option 2) is unrelated to safety screening. Relying only on the primary model's refusals (option 3) provides no defense-in-depth for downstream actions.
referenceText: Use a fast Claude-based classifier as a moderation stage to screen user inputs before downstream processing.

stem: A hospital network is building a clinical-note summarization feature on the Claude API. Prompts will contain protected health information (PHI), and the deployment must satisfy HIPAA. As the solution architect, which two governance controls are essential before PHI is processed? (Select 2.)
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and provision a HIPAA-enabled organization so PHI is processed only under contractual coverage with the required lifecycle safeguards, and retained data is not used to train models.
Apply data minimization by sending only the minimum necessary PHI in each prompt, and enforce role-based access controls with audit logging over every request.
Rely on Claude's built-in safety guardrails to automatically detect and strip all PHI from prompts before inference.
Persist complete prompts and responses containing PHI indefinitely in plaintext application logs to guarantee a full audit trail.
Fine-tune a dedicated Claude model on the network's historical PHI so that patient data never leaves an internal training pipeline.
rationale: HIPAA readiness for a covered entity rests on two architect-controlled pillars. Option 0 is correct: a BAA is the contractual mechanism that permits a business associate to handle PHI, and Anthropic pairs it with a HIPAA-enabled organization that enforces feature eligibility and applies encryption, access controls, and audit logging protecting PHI throughout its lifecycle; retained data is not used for training without express permission. Note that per Anthropic's docs, HIPAA readiness is an alternative to zero data retention (ZDR)-you do not configure ZDR for PHI, because covered HIPAA workloads retain data with safeguards rather than requiring immediate deletion. Option 1 is correct: the minimum-necessary standard (data minimization) plus access controls and audit logging are core HIPAA safeguards and are the customer's responsibility, not the model's. Option 2 is wrong because Claude's safety features are not a certified PHI-detection or de-identification control and must never be trusted to sanitize regulated data. Option 3 is wrong because indefinitely retaining PHI in plaintext logs violates minimization and retention principles and expands breach exposure rather than satisfying audit needs. Option 4 is wrong because fine-tuning on PHI is not a required control, is not offered for Claude in this manner, and does not itself establish a lawful basis or the required agreements and safeguards.
referenceText: Anthropic's API and data retention documentation describes HIPAA readiness: a signed BAA plus a HIPAA-enabled organization applies encryption, access controls, and audit logging that protect PHI throughout its lifecycle. HIPAA readiness is an alternative to ZDR rather than combined with it, and retained data is never used for training without express permission. The minimum-necessary standard and access/audit controls are HIPAA safeguards the customer must implement.

stem: An EU-based SaaS company logs every Claude API prompt and response into an observability platform with a three-year retention window. Some logged prompts contain customer personal data. A customer submits a GDPR right-to-erasure request. Which design change best aligns the logging pipeline with GDPR data minimization and erasure obligations?
options: Minimize the personal data captured in logs, tag records with a data-subject identifier, and set retention limits so specific records can be located and deleted when an erasure request arrives.
Retain all logs for the full three years regardless of erasure requests, because immutable audit trails always take precedence over individual deletion rights.
Stop logging prompts and responses entirely so no personal data is ever stored, eliminating any need for retention or deletion controls.
Rely on the model provider to erase the customer's data from Claude's memory, since the model retains past prompts and can be instructed to forget them.
rationale: Option 0 is correct: GDPR requires data minimization, purpose-limited retention, and the ability to locate and delete a specific data subject's personal data on request. Tagging records to a subject identifier and enforcing retention limits makes both observability and erasure feasible. Option 1 is wrong: the absolute claim that audit trails 'always' override erasure is false; controllers must balance legal-retention bases against erasure rights and cannot categorically refuse. Option 2 is wrong because eliminating all logging is an over-engineered response that destroys legitimate observability and troubleshooting; minimization means collecting only what is necessary, not nothing. Option 3 is wrong because it reflects a misconception: the Claude API is stateless and does not 'remember' prior prompts, so erasure must be handled in the customer's own storage systems, not by asking the model to forget.
referenceText: Anthropic's Privacy Center describes its approach to GDPR, including its role as a data processor under a Data Processing Addendum. The right to erasure, data minimization, and storage-limitation obligations are core GDPR principles that the data controller must implement in its own logging and retention design, since the Claude API does not retain prompts as model state.

stem: A production application is pinned to a specific Claude model snapshot. Anthropic publishes a deprecation notice for that snapshot with a retirement date 6 months out. As the architect, what is the most responsible lifecycle action to communicate and execute with stakeholders?
options: Wait until the retirement date, then swap the model ID in production, since behavior is guaranteed identical across snapshots
Immediately hard-pin to the newest model without evaluation, because newer snapshots are always strictly better
Schedule a migration plan that re-runs the regression eval suite against the successor model, communicates a cutover timeline to stakeholders, and completes the swap before the retirement date
Remove the explicit model ID so requests float to whatever the API defaults to, eliminating future migration work
rationale: The correct approach treats deprecation as a planned lifecycle event: validate the successor with the existing eval suite, communicate a timeline, and cut over before retirement. Waiting until the deadline risks an outage and assumes identical behavior, which is not guaranteed. Blindly pinning the newest model without evaluation ignores possible regressions. Dropping the model ID to float on defaults sacrifices reproducibility and can cause silent behavior changes at any time.
referenceText: Anthropic provides deprecation notices and retirement dates for models so teams can plan migrations; pin explicit model versions and test before migrating.

stem: A finance stakeholder asks why the monthly Claude bill dropped 40% after your team enabled prompt caching on a workload that repeatedly sends a large, static system prompt. Which explanation is accurate?
options: Cache reads are billed at a reduced rate compared with processing the same input tokens uncached, so repeated large static prefixes cost less on subsequent calls
Prompt caching makes all input and output tokens free after the first request
Prompt caching reduces cost by automatically switching the request to a smaller model
Prompt caching only reduces latency and has no effect on token billing
rationale: Cache reads (cache hits) are billed at a fraction of the normal input token price, so a large repeated static prefix becomes much cheaper on subsequent calls; there is also a one-time cache-write premium. It does not make tokens free, does not change the model, and it affects both latency and cost, not latency alone.
referenceText: Cache read tokens are billed at a reduced rate relative to base input tokens; cache writes carry a one-time premium.

stem: A stakeholder wants to process 2 million overnight classification jobs at the lowest cost, and the results are not needed in real time. Which Claude capability should the architect propose, and what expectation must be set?
options: The Message Batches API, setting the expectation that results are returned asynchronously (typically within 24 hours) at a discounted per-token rate
The standard synchronous Messages API, promising sub-second latency per request at the batch discount
Prompt caching alone, promising the full batch completes in real time at no token cost
Streaming responses, promising the batch discount applies to every streamed token
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large asynchronous, latency-tolerant workloads and offers a substantial discount, with results typically completing within 24 hours. Synchronous Messages, prompt caching, and streaming do not provide the batch discount and do not fit a not-real-time bulk workload the way batching does.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at a reduced cost, with batches completing within 24 hours.

stem: You are presenting a model right-sizing recommendation to stakeholders for a new feature. Which factors are legitimate inputs to a defensible model-selection decision? (Select 3.)
options: The measured accuracy of each candidate model on a task-specific evaluation set
The per-request latency and cost budget the feature must meet
The complexity of the reasoning the task actually requires
Choosing the largest model in every case because bigger is always safer
Picking a model based solely on which one a competitor publicly mentioned
rationale: Right-sizing balances task-specific eval accuracy, latency/cost constraints, and the intrinsic complexity of the task. Defaulting to the largest model in all cases wastes cost and latency where a smaller model suffices, and copying a competitor's public choice ignores your own requirements and data.
referenceText: Choose a model by balancing capability, speed, and cost against the requirements of your specific task, validated with your own evaluations.

stem: Before a product launch, stakeholders ask how you will know the Claude feature is good enough to ship. What is the strongest lifecycle practice to agree on with them first?
options: Define measurable success criteria and build a representative evaluation set with graded cases before development, then gate the launch on hitting the target
Ship first and rely on user complaints as the primary evaluation signal
Judge quality informally by having one engineer read a handful of outputs the morning of launch
Assume quality is acceptable because the prompt reads well to the author
rationale: Agreeing on measurable success criteria and an eval set up front turns 'good enough' into an objective, stakeholder-approved gate. Waiting for complaints, spot-reading a few outputs, or trusting author intuition are unrepresentative and non-repeatable, and expose the launch to undetected regressions.
referenceText: Define success criteria that are measurable and build evaluations that reflect real-world usage before iterating on prompts.

stem: An SRE stakeholder wants production observability for your Claude integration so incidents can be diagnosed quickly. Which signals are most valuable to capture and monitor? (Select 3.)
options: Latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per model and endpoint
Error and rate-limit response rates over time
Token usage per request broken down by input, output, and cache hits
Only the total number of API calls, with no per-request detail
The raw API key value logged with each request for traceability
rationale: Latency percentiles, error/rate-limit rates, and token breakdowns (including cache hits) are the core operational signals for diagnosing latency, reliability, and cost issues. A single aggregate call count lacks the granularity to diagnose anything, and logging raw API keys is a serious credential-leak security violation, not an observability practice.
referenceText: Monitor latency, error rates, and token usage; rate-limit and error headers help diagnose and back off appropriately.

stem: A healthcare stakeholder must confirm the deployment can handle protected health information (PHI) under HIPAA. What is the correct governance step before sending PHI to Claude?
options: Execute a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with the provider and confirm the specific service/deployment is in scope before transmitting PHI
Rely on TLS encryption in transit alone, since encryption satisfies all HIPAA obligations
Send PHI immediately because Anthropic's models never retain any input data under any configuration
Ask each patient to individually approve the API calls at request time
rationale: HIPAA requires a signed BAA covering the specific in-scope service before PHI is processed by a business associate. Encryption in transit is necessary but not sufficient; a blanket claim about zero retention is not a substitute for a BAA and contractual scoping; and per-request patient approval is not the mechanism HIPAA compliance relies on.
referenceText: Customers handling PHI should execute a Business Associate Agreement and use services within the agreed compliance scope.

stem: A privacy/legal stakeholder requires the RAG pipeline to respect GDPR data-minimization principles. Which design choices support that requirement? (Select 2.)
options: Only retrieve and pass to the model the minimum document chunks relevant to the user's query, rather than the entire corpus
Redact or exclude personal data fields that are not needed to answer the query before sending context to the model
Embed and store every document in full, including unused personal data, to simplify future retrieval
Send the user's entire account history with every request regardless of relevance to improve answer quality
rationale: Data minimization means processing only the personal data necessary for the purpose. Retrieving just the relevant chunks and redacting unnecessary personal fields both limit exposure. Storing full documents with unused personal data and attaching entire histories to every request maximize rather than minimize processed personal data, contradicting GDPR.
referenceText: Send only the context needed to answer the query; minimize the personal data included in prompts and retrieval.

stem: A compliance stakeholder needs an auditable record of who created API keys and changed workspace settings across your organization's Anthropic account. Which capability should the architect point them to?
options: The Admin API, which programmatically exposes organization members, workspaces, and API key management for audit and governance
The Messages API response body, which contains a full administrative audit trail by default
Prompt caching metrics, which record all administrative configuration changes
The model deprecation notices, which list every user action in the account
rationale: The Admin API is the governance surface for managing and auditing members, workspaces, and API keys at the organization level. The Messages API response, prompt caching metrics, and deprecation notices are unrelated to administrative audit trails.
referenceText: The Admin API lets organizations programmatically manage workspaces, members, and API keys for governance and auditing.

stem: You are designing a resilient client for the Claude API and must set stakeholder expectations for degraded conditions. Which practices belong in the resilience design? (Select 3.)
options: Retry transient 429 and 5xx responses using exponential backoff with jitter
Set sensible request timeouts and surface a graceful fallback when a call exceeds them
Honor the retry-after guidance from rate-limit responses rather than retrying immediately in a tight loop
Retry 4xx validation errors indefinitely until they succeed
Disable timeouts entirely so no request is ever cut off
rationale: Exponential backoff with jitter, sensible timeouts with graceful fallback, and honoring retry-after are standard resilience patterns for transient failures. Retrying deterministic 4xx validation errors will never succeed and wastes quota, and disabling timeouts lets requests hang indefinitely, harming reliability rather than improving it.
referenceText: Handle 429 and 5xx errors with retries and exponential backoff; respect retry-after and avoid retrying non-retryable 4xx errors.

stem: Your service has a hard SLA and stakeholders want continuity if the primary model is temporarily unavailable or rate-limited. Which fallback design best balances resilience with quality expectations?
options: Define a documented fallback to an alternate model or region, validate it against the eval suite, and communicate any expected quality/latency differences to stakeholders
Return a hardcoded canned response whenever the primary model errors, and tell stakeholders quality is unchanged
Retry the primary model in a tight loop with no backoff until it responds, regardless of latency budget
Silently drop requests that fail the first attempt so error rates look low
rationale: A validated, documented fallback path (alternate model or region) with communicated trade-offs preserves availability while managing quality expectations honestly. Canned responses misrepresent quality, tight retry loops blow the latency budget and can worsen rate limiting, and silently dropping requests hides failures from stakeholders and users.
referenceText: Design for graceful degradation with validated fallbacks; Claude is available across the API, Bedrock, and Vertex AI for redundancy.

stem: A procurement stakeholder asks whether to consume Claude via the Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, or Google Vertex AI. Which statements about this lifecycle/deployment choice are accurate? (Select 2.)
options: Bedrock and Vertex let organizations consume Claude within their existing AWS or Google Cloud accounts, billing, and IAM/governance controls
Model IDs and some regional availability differ across the Anthropic API, Bedrock, and Vertex, so integration code and configuration must account for the platform
Choosing Bedrock or Vertex means the Messages API request/response format is completely incompatible and must be rewritten from scratch
Only the first-party Anthropic API can ever access any Claude model; Bedrock and Vertex expose no Claude models
rationale: Bedrock and Vertex let teams use Claude within their existing cloud account, billing, and governance, and model identifiers and regional availability differ by platform, so configuration must be platform-aware. The message format is largely consistent across platforms (SDK support exists), and Bedrock and Vertex do expose Claude models, so the last two options are false.
referenceText: Claude is available through Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI, using platform-specific model identifiers and regions within your cloud account.

stem: A non-technical stakeholder asks why your team adopted the Model Context Protocol (MCP) instead of writing bespoke integrations for each data source and tool. What is the clearest, accurate justification?
options: MCP is an open standard that lets models connect to tools and data sources through a common interface, reducing per-integration custom glue and improving reuse
MCP is a proprietary Anthropic-only wire format that locks all integrations to a single vendor
MCP replaces the need for any evaluation because standardized connectors are guaranteed bug-free
MCP is a billing feature that discounts token usage for connected tools
rationale: MCP is an open standard providing a common interface between models and external tools/data, cutting bespoke integration work and enabling reuse across clients and servers. It is not proprietary or single-vendor, it does not remove the need for evaluation, and it is not a billing/discount mechanism.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open standard for connecting AI applications to external tools and data through a common interface.

stem: Business stakeholders in a regulated domain are worried that RAG answers cannot be trusted because sources are unclear. Which Claude capability most directly addresses this concern and how should you frame its value?
options: The Citations feature, which lets Claude ground responses in provided documents and return references to the exact source passages, improving verifiability and auditability
Prompt caching, which guarantees that every cited fact is correct because the context is cached
Streaming, which proves answer correctness because tokens arrive incrementally
The Batches API, which attaches legal sign-off to every generated answer
rationale: Citations grounds responses in supplied documents and returns pointers to the specific source passages, giving stakeholders verifiable, auditable answers. Prompt caching, streaming, and batching are performance/cost features and provide no source attribution or correctness guarantee.
referenceText: Citations enables Claude to ground its answers in source documents and provide references to the specific passages used.

stem: As part of lifecycle/change management, your team edits the production system prompt frequently. A stakeholder is concerned about undetected quality regressions from these edits. What is the best safeguard to institute?
options: Version-control every prompt change and run the regression eval suite in CI before promoting a new prompt to production
Edit the production prompt directly and rely on individual engineers to remember what changed
Freeze the prompt permanently so it can never be improved
Only test prompt changes after a customer files a support ticket
rationale: Treating prompts as versioned artifacts gated by an eval suite in CI catches regressions before release and gives stakeholders an auditable change history. Direct edits with human memory are error-prone, permanent freezing blocks improvement, and reacting only to tickets means regressions reach users first.
referenceText: Iterate on prompts against a held-out evaluation set and track changes so regressions are caught before deployment.

stem: A finance stakeholder wants a recurring, itemized view of Claude spend by workspace to attribute cost to teams. Which approach should the architect recommend?
options: Use the Usage and Cost API (Admin API) to programmatically pull usage and cost data broken down by workspace for reporting
Estimate cost by multiplying total request count by a flat per-request price, ignoring token counts
Read cost from the Messages API response body, which itemizes dollar spend per workspace
Rely on the prompt caching documentation page as the source of live spend data
rationale: The Usage and Cost API exposes usage and cost data that can be broken down by workspace, enabling accurate chargeback reporting. A flat per-request estimate ignores variable token usage, the Messages response does not report dollar spend, and a docs page is not a live data source.
referenceText: The Usage and Cost API provides programmatic access to token usage and cost data, filterable by workspace and model.

stem: A Claude-powered service has a production incident (elevated error rates). What belongs in effective stakeholder incident communication during and after the event? (Select 3.)
options: A timely status update describing user-facing impact and the current mitigation
An estimated time to resolution or next-update time, revised as understanding improves
A post-incident review capturing root cause and preventive actions
Silence until the incident is fully resolved to avoid alarming stakeholders
Assigning individual blame to a named engineer in the public update
rationale: Effective incident communication provides timely impact/mitigation updates, an ETA or next-update commitment, and a blameless post-incident review with preventive actions. Staying silent leaves stakeholders uninformed and erodes trust, and public individual blame damages culture without improving reliability.
referenceText: Communicate impact, mitigation, and expected next updates during incidents, and follow up with a root-cause review and preventive actions.

stem: During a RAG design review, a stakeholder challenges your chunking strategy. Which justification reflects sound, documentable chunking practice?
options: Chunk sizes and overlap were chosen to preserve semantic coherence and fit retrieval/context constraints, and were validated by measuring retrieval quality on an eval set
Chunks were fixed at one character each to maximize the number of retrievable units
The entire corpus is passed as a single chunk on every query so nothing is ever missed
Chunk boundaries were placed at random byte offsets to avoid bias
rationale: Good chunking preserves semantic units, respects retrieval and context-window limits, and is validated empirically by retrieval-quality metrics. One-character chunks destroy meaning, a single whole-corpus chunk defeats retrieval and blows the context budget, and random byte-offset boundaries fragment meaning arbitrarily.
referenceText: Chunk documents to preserve semantic meaning and fit context limits, and validate retrieval quality empirically.

stem: A stakeholder asks how much advance warning your team will have before a model you depend on stops working, so they can plan budget and engineering time. What is the accurate lifecycle expectation to communicate?
options: Anthropic publishes deprecation notices with retirement dates in advance, so the team subscribes to and tracks these notices to plan migrations before retirement
Models are removed without any notice, so no planning is possible
A model can never be retired once released, so no migration will ever be required
Retirement dates apply only to the free tier and never affect paid production usage
rationale: Anthropic publishes deprecation and retirement information in advance, and tracking it lets teams plan migrations proactively. Models are not removed without notice, they can be retired over time, and retirement is not limited to a free tier.
referenceText: Deprecated models have published retirement dates; monitor deprecation notices to plan and complete migrations in advance.

stem: For a high-visibility launch, stakeholders want to limit blast radius if the new Claude feature underperforms in production. Which rollout approach best fits lifecycle risk management?
options: A phased/canary rollout that exposes a small traffic percentage first, monitors quality and error metrics against agreed thresholds, and expands only when gates pass
A full 100% cutover on day one, because staged rollouts merely delay inevitable issues
Enabling the feature only in a local developer environment and never in production, then declaring the launch complete
Rolling out to all users but disabling monitoring to reduce operational overhead
rationale: A canary/phased rollout with monitored gates limits blast radius and gives stakeholders objective go/no-go signals before full exposure. A same-day full cutover maximizes risk, a dev-only 'launch' never reaches users, and rolling out with monitoring disabled removes the very signals needed to catch problems.
referenceText: Roll out changes gradually and monitor evaluation and operational metrics before expanding to all traffic.

stem: An architect receives an Anthropic notice that a Claude model the company relies on in production has entered a deprecation period with a scheduled retirement date. Which action best reflects sound lifecycle management when communicating this to engineering and business stakeholders?
options: Immediately disable the affected endpoints so no traffic hits a deprecated model before the retirement date
Publish a migration plan with the retirement date, the recommended successor model, and a regression-testing window before cutover
Wait until Anthropic returns retirement errors, then swap the model ID in production as a hotfix
Instruct stakeholders that deprecated models never change behavior, so no re-evaluation is required before migrating
rationale: Anthropic's deprecation process gives a defined notice period and a recommended replacement so teams can plan; the right response is a communicated migration plan that dates the cutover and reserves time to re-run evaluations against the successor model. Disabling endpoints immediately causes an unnecessary outage while the model still works during the deprecation window. Waiting for retirement errors turns a planned migration into an incident. Claiming deprecated models require no re-evaluation is false and dangerous—successor models can differ in behavior, which is exactly why regression testing is needed.
referenceText: Anthropic model deprecations policy and lifecycle stages

stem: Before rolling a newly released Claude model into a customer-facing product, a product owner asks the architect how to reduce the risk that stakeholders experience unexpected quality changes at launch. Which approach best manages that risk?
options: Route 100% of traffic to the new model on day one and monitor support tickets for regressions
Run the new model against a held-out evaluation set, then do a canary rollout to a small traffic percentage before full cutover
Pin the old model indefinitely and decline all future model upgrades to guarantee stability
Rely on the model card benchmark scores alone and skip application-specific evaluation
rationale: Managing a model upgrade means validating against your own evaluation suite (which reflects your task) and then exposing a small slice of traffic first so regressions surface before they reach everyone. Full immediate cutover makes support tickets your test harness. Pinning forever ignores deprecation timelines and forfeits improvements. Published benchmarks do not capture application-specific behavior, so they cannot replace your own evals as the go/no-go signal.
referenceText: Building and running application-specific evaluations before deployment

stem: A finance stakeholder wants a monthly forecast of Claude API spend and asks the architect which levers most directly explain cost. Which factor is the primary driver the architect should surface in cost reporting?
options: The number of distinct API keys provisioned across teams
The count of input and output tokens processed, priced per model tier
The number of HTTP 429 rate-limit responses received
The geographic region from which requests originate
rationale: Anthropic bills by input and output tokens at per-model rates, so token volume times model price is the honest basis for a spend forecast. The number of API keys does not itself incur cost. Rate-limit 429s are throttling signals, not billed usage. Region is not the pricing dimension. Grounding stakeholder cost conversations in tokens-per-model keeps forecasts defensible and connects directly to optimization levers like model right-sizing and prompt caching.
referenceText: Token counting and how usage maps to cost

stem: An architect is drafting a stakeholder communication justifying a plan to cut Claude inference cost on a large nightly document-processing job without degrading output quality. Which techniques are legitimate cost levers to include? (Select 2.)
options: Submit the workload through the Message Batches API for its lower per-token pricing
Right-size to a smaller Claude model only after evaluations confirm it meets the quality bar
Lower the temperature parameter to reduce the number of tokens billed
Remove the anthropic-version header so requests default to an untracked pricing tier
Disable retries entirely so failed requests are never re-billed
rationale: The Message Batches API offers reduced pricing for asynchronous, non-time-sensitive workloads like a nightly batch, making it a real cost lever. Right-sizing to a smaller model is legitimate specifically when evals confirm quality holds. Temperature controls sampling randomness, not token count or price. Removing the version header does not create a cheaper tier and breaks version pinning. Disabling retries harms resilience and does not meaningfully change cost, since successful requests are billed regardless.
referenceText: Message Batches API pricing and asynchronous processing

stem: A compliance stakeholder asks the architect to confirm how the team pins the exact model and API behavior so an audited workload does not silently change between releases. Which practice provides that guarantee?
options: Always using an alias like the latest snapshot so the newest fixes apply automatically
Specifying a dated model snapshot ID and a fixed anthropic-version header on every request
Relying on the account default model configured in the console
Caching responses so identical prompts never re-invoke the model
rationale: Reproducibility for audit means pinning both the specific dated model snapshot and the anthropic-version header, so neither the model weights nor the API contract shift underneath you. A latest alias intentionally floats and can change behavior without notice. Account-default configuration is mutable and not request-level explicit. Response caching avoids re-invocation for identical inputs but does nothing to pin behavior for new inputs, which is the audit concern.
referenceText: API versioning and the anthropic-version header

stem: During an incident review, stakeholders want the architect to explain how the system should behave when the Claude API returns transient 529 (overloaded) responses under load. Which resilient design should the architect communicate as the standard?
options: Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request succeeds
Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, and fall back to a degraded response after a bounded number of attempts
Treat every 529 as a permanent failure and surface an error to the user on the first occurrence
Increase the request timeout to several minutes so the overload resolves itself
rationale: Overloaded responses are transient, so exponential backoff with jitter avoids synchronized retry storms, and a bounded attempt count with a graceful fallback keeps the user experience predictable. Tight immediate retry loops amplify the overload. Treating a transient 529 as permanent needlessly fails recoverable requests. Simply extending timeouts holds connections open without addressing the retry strategy and worsens latency for users.
referenceText: API error types, retries, and handling overloaded responses

stem: A healthcare customer's stakeholders ask whether the architecture can meet HIPAA obligations when using Claude. Which statement most accurately frames what the architect should communicate?
options: Claude output is inherently HIPAA-compliant, so no additional controls are required
Compliance depends on the deployment: a Business Associate Agreement plus data-handling controls such as minimization and audit logging must be in place
Sending PHI is acceptable as long as the prompt asks Claude to keep it confidential
HIPAA only applies to data at rest, so encrypting the database is sufficient
rationale: HIPAA compliance is a property of the whole system and contractual posture, not the model alone: it requires a BAA covering PHI plus operational controls like data minimization and audit logging. No model is automatically compliant. A prompt instruction to keep data confidential is not a safeguard. HIPAA covers data in transit, in use, and access controls, not just encryption at rest, so encrypting the database alone is insufficient.
referenceText: Anthropic compliance, data handling, and enterprise agreements

stem: Legal stakeholders reviewing a RAG assistant want assurance that end users can trace answers back to source documents. Which Claude capability should the architect point to, and how should its limits be communicated?
options: Enable the Citations feature so responses reference supplied source passages, while noting citations reduce but do not eliminate the need for human review
Instruct the model in the system prompt to append a bibliography, guaranteeing every claim is grounded
Raise max_tokens so the model has room to quote entire documents verbatim in every answer
Rely on the model's training data recall, since Claude always cites the documents it was trained on
rationale: The Citations feature makes Claude ground responses in the document passages you provide and return references to them, which is the right traceability mechanism—paired with the honest caveat that it lowers but does not remove the need for human verification. A prompt-appended bibliography does not guarantee grounding and can be fabricated. Inflating max_tokens to quote whole documents is wasteful and off-point. Claude does not cite its training data, so relying on recall is incorrect.
referenceText: Citations feature for grounding responses in provided sources

stem: An architect is defining what should appear on a shared observability dashboard so that operations, product, and finance stakeholders each get the signal they need. Which metrics are appropriate to expose? (Select 3.)
options: End-to-end latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) per endpoint
Input and output token consumption trended over time
Rate of API errors and retries by status code
The full plaintext of every prompt and completion for all users
The random seed used internally by the model for each request
rationale: Latency percentiles serve operations and user-experience SLAs, token consumption trends serve finance and cost forecasting, and error/retry rates serve reliability monitoring—these are the cross-stakeholder signals a dashboard should carry. Exposing full plaintext prompts and completions for all users on a shared dashboard violates data-minimization and privacy principles. There is no user-controllable per-request seed exposed as an operational metric, so it is not a meaningful dashboard signal.
referenceText: Latency measurement, monitoring, and reducing latency

stem: A stakeholder asks why the architect chose to run a specific regulated workload on Claude via Amazon Bedrock rather than calling the Anthropic API directly. Which justification is most defensible to communicate?
options: Bedrock runs a newer, higher-quality version of Claude that the Anthropic API cannot access
Bedrock lets the workload stay within the customer's existing AWS account, IAM, and compliance boundary, easing governance sign-off
Bedrock removes all token-based pricing, making the workload effectively free
Bedrock guarantees zero latency because inference happens on the customer's own hardware
